correct.why: Test criteria must be agreed by stakeholders, and any change goes through change control.

distractors.text: Change the test criteria so validation testing can start as scheduled.
distractors.trap: Changing criteria requires change control approval first.

distractors.text: Keep the test criteria unchanged because a change would affect the schedule.
distractors.trap: Ignoring valid concerns can compromise product quality.

distractors.text: Explain the impact of change and persuade the test lead to accept the original criteria.
distractors.trap: Pressuring agreement ignores a potentially legitimate quality issue.

explanation: The project manager should understand why the test lead disagrees and, if warranted, submit a change request to the change control board. Criteria cannot be altered without approval, and dismissing the concern risks product quality.

prompts: A project manager is working with remote delivery specialists who are acting independently of one another. What should the project manager do to promote collaboration among them?

correct.text: Promote regular meetings using video conferencing.
correct.why: Face-to-face video interaction fosters real collaboration among dispersed team members.

distractors.text: Include all delivery specialists on every project-related email.
distractors.trap: Broad email copying shares information but does not build collaboration.

distractors.text: Call delivery specialists individually each week to gather and share updates.
distractors.trap: One-on-one calls keep members isolated rather than collaborating.

distractors.text: Ask each team member to complete a daily task log for updates.
distractors.trap: Task logs track work but do not connect the team.

explanation: Regular video conferencing gives dispersed team members richer, face-to-face interaction, which promotes genuine collaboration far better than emails, individual calls, or task logs.

eco_task: Engage Stakeholders and Build Shared Understanding
topic: Engage Stakeholders and Build Shared Understanding
prompts: A project manager has just been assigned to an existing multinational project and is not yet familiar with the key stakeholders. Which response is best?

correct.text: Review and update the stakeholder register.
correct.why: The stakeholder register captures each stakeholder's role and attitude, the fastest way to learn them.

distractors.text: Request a meeting with the resource manager.
distractors.trap: The resource manager will not know all project stakeholders.

distractors.text: Seek the expert judgment of the sponsor.
distractors.trap: The sponsor's judgment will not teach the PM about all stakeholders.

distractors.text: Identify the communications tool and update the plan.
distractors.trap: Updating the communication plan does not help the PM learn the stakeholders.

explanation: The stakeholder register documents the project's stakeholders, their roles, and their attitudes, making it the most direct way for a new project manager to familiarize themselves with the key stakeholders.

prompts: Midway through a release, the project manager is replaced. The team tells the new project manager about friction caused by the previous manager's constant demands for rapid delivery. What approach should the new project manager take to quickly establish credibility with the team?

correct.text: Assess the team's culture and observe and interact with them.
correct.why: Understanding how the team works and how to serve them builds credibility fastest.

distractors.text: Do what the team needs done, maintain the action log, and provide feedback.
distractors.trap: Routine service is implicit and does not quickly build credibility.

distractors.text: Help the team succeed with excellent service, on-time commitments, and adequate budget.
distractors.trap: This is baseline PM duty, not a way to rapidly earn trust.

distractors.text: Have the team explain what is needed, create tasks, and report progress.
distractors.trap: Task-and-report mechanics ignore the team's conflicting needs and culture.

explanation: To quickly establish credibility, the new project manager should first observe and understand how the team works and how best to serve them, ensuring any changes align with the team's culture and desired outcomes.

prompts: During a standup, a team member reports a story as complete when it is not. Because other stories depend on it, the team is blocked when they try to build on it. What should the project manager do first?

correct.text: Speak privately with the team member who reported the story as complete.
correct.why: Addressing the person directly resolves the immediate issue and surfaces why the false report occurred.

distractors.text: Review iteration planning with the whole team to map the story interdependencies.
distractors.trap: Jumps to a systemic fix before understanding why the individual misreported.

distractors.text: Have the product owner review acceptance criteria before items enter the sprint backlog.
distractors.trap: Shifts blame to intake controls rather than the reporting behavior.

distractors.text: Ask the team to explain their interpretation of the definition of done at sprint end.
distractors.trap: Delays action to retrospective and treats it as a team-wide misunderstanding.

explanation: The other steps may be worthwhile later, but the immediate priority is a private conversation with the individual to understand and correct the false status report.

prompts: After emailing a status report, the project manager learns several recipients are no longer involved with the project. To correct the distribution list for future reports, what should the project manager do?

correct.text: Review and update the stakeholder communications approach.
correct.why: This is the artifact that defines who receives which reports, so it drives the distribution list.

distractors.text: Review and update the schedule management plan.
distractors.trap: Governs scheduling, not who should receive status reports.

distractors.text: Review and update the responsibility assignment matrix.
distractors.trap: Maps task responsibility, not report recipients.

distractors.text: Review and update the resource management plan.
distractors.trap: Defines resource needs, not communication recipients.

explanation: The stakeholder communications approach identifies who receives project communications, so it is the artifact to review and update to fix the distribution list.

eco_task: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
topic: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
prompts: A long-running project has exhausted the team, and deadlines prevent the project manager from extending the schedule. What should the project manager do next?

correct.text: Discuss the situation openly with the team.
correct.why: The team hears their concerns and often has ideas to improve efficiency or reduce workload.

distractors.text: Review the project charter.
distractors.trap: Useful background but does not address the team's exhaustion now.

distractors.text: Offer more recognition to the delivery specialists.
distractors.trap: Recognition alone will not relieve an overloaded team.

distractors.text: Request additional resources.
distractors.trap: Not guaranteed to be available or to solve the workload problem.

explanation: Since the schedule cannot move, the project manager discusses the situation with the team, who feel heard and may offer ways to work more efficiently or lighten the load.

prompts: A team spread across three countries struggles to arrange meetings, and specialists are unclear about requirements and unable to get questions answered. What should be done to improve project success?

correct.text: Review the stakeholder communications approach and increase information sharing.
correct.why: Better, more documented communication ensures distributed members understand the requirements.

distractors.text: Have members work independently until the work must be integrated.
distractors.trap: Independent work fails when requirements are unclear.

distractors.text: Increase the frequency of team meetings to answer questions.
distractors.trap: More meetings help little when scheduling is already difficult.

distractors.text: Route questions to functional managers to resolve across the team.
distractors.trap: Offloads work to functional managers unnecessarily.

explanation: For distributed teams, improving and documenting communication makes requirements understood by all; relying on more meetings or verbal-only channels is impractical when scheduling is hard.

eco_task: Engage Stakeholders and Build Shared Understanding
topic: Engage Stakeholders and Build Shared Understanding
prompts: During an executive oversight meeting, the project manager reports that a previously supportive key team member has become resistant and negative toward changes. Where should this information be captured?

correct.text: Stakeholder engagement assessment matrix
correct.why: It records each stakeholder's current versus desired engagement, the exact detail described.

distractors.text: Responsibility assignment (RACI) matrix
distractors.trap: Defines roles only, not engagement or attitude.

distractors.text: Risk log and issue tracker
distractors.trap: Captures risks and issues, not a stakeholder's engagement level.

distractors.text: Work performance reports
distractors.trap: Report on the work itself, not individual attitudes.

explanation: A shift in a stakeholder's attitude is engagement information, recorded in the stakeholder engagement assessment matrix, which tracks current versus desired engagement.

prompts: After the sponsor approves the charter, the project manager presents it to stakeholders and notices some are disengaged during the meeting. What should the project manager do?

correct.text: Hold one-on-one meetings with these stakeholders to confirm their understanding of key deliverables and responsibilities.
correct.why: Direct engagement is the project manager's job and resolves the disengagement immediately.

distractors.text: Log it in the risk register because it is unclear whether they understood their responsibilities.
distractors.trap: Treats a current engagement issue as a future risk.

distractors.text: Report the lack of involvement to the executive sponsor.
distractors.trap: Escalates instead of the project manager resolving it directly.

distractors.text: Ask the project team to monitor these stakeholders' involvement.
distractors.trap: Delegates a responsibility that belongs to the project manager.

explanation: Every stakeholder must understand the deliverables and their responsibilities; the project manager addresses the disengagement directly through one-on-one meetings rather than logging or escalating it.

eco_task: Engage Stakeholders and Build Shared Understanding
topic: Engage Stakeholders and Build Shared Understanding
prompts: A project manager forms a team of new and experienced specialists and finds collaboration issues: the experienced members believe the newer ones lack the needed technical skills and expect to compensate for them. What should the project manager do?

correct.text: Assess the skills of all specialists, identify gaps, and plan to address them.
correct.why: Assessing everyone reveals real strengths and gaps and gives all members an equal path to grow.

distractors.text: Offer bonuses to experienced specialists who mentor the new ones.
distractors.trap: Ignores the newer members' skills and can breed resentment.

distractors.text: Reassign the new specialists to projects better matching their experience.
distractors.trap: Dismisses the new members without evaluating their abilities.

distractors.text: Explain that professionals are expected to resolve issues without intervention.
distractors.trap: Abdicates the project manager's duty to support the team.

explanation: Experience does not guarantee proficiency, so the project manager assesses all members' competencies and closes gaps through training and coaching, fostering collaboration and equal growth.

prompts: An adaptive team is globally distributed with members speaking different first languages, meeting virtually in a common language. Differences in pronunciation, accent, and vocabulary make it hard for some members to understand each other. What should the project manager tailor to improve the daily meeting?

correct.text: Use an electronic task board to give a visual representation of work status and progress.
correct.why: A visual display conveys status regardless of language differences, reducing misunderstanding.

distractors.text: Verbally summarize each member's reported work status during the call.
distractors.trap: Adds more speech, the very source of the comprehension problem.

distractors.text: Request a budget change to provide a translation service.
distractors.trap: Costly, may be infeasible, and can add distortion and delay.

distractors.text: Ask all members to report completed work, upcoming work, and blockers.
distractors.trap: Standard verbal reporting does not overcome the language barrier.

explanation: A shared electronic task board gives a visual summary of progress that everyone can follow despite language differences, where verbal reporting or translation does not solve comprehension.

prompts: A project manager is unsure whether the team has the critical skills to deliver a key feature of an upcoming deliverable. To reduce the risk of sprint failure, what should the project manager do?

correct.text: Identify the potential skill gap and propose addressing it with training, mentoring, or coaching.
correct.why: Servant leaders develop the existing team by closing identified skill gaps proactively.

distractors.text: Ask the sponsor to replace low performers with specialists who have the skill set.
distractors.trap: Disruptive and ignores developing the current team.

distractors.text: Negotiate to delay the key feature to a later release while finding an alternative.
distractors.trap: Postpones rather than resolving the skill gap.

distractors.text: Accept the sprint failure and motivate the team to do better next time.
distractors.trap: Passive acceptance of a risk that could cause failure.

explanation: As a servant leader, the project manager first identifies the skill gap, then proposes closing it through training, mentoring, or coaching to develop the existing team and reduce risk.

eco_task: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
topic: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
prompts: On a midsize project building an innovative product with changing requirements, several specialists are unavailable two weeks each month. The cost of quality rises sharply during those periods, though team velocity stays stable. What should the project manager do?

correct.text: Offer cross-training.
correct.why: Cross-training lets members cover for absent specialists, closing the skill gap that raises quality costs.

distractors.text: Add resources to the team.
distractors.trap: May be infeasible and does not address the quality-cost cause.

distractors.text: Expand the testing effort.
distractors.trap: Does not resolve the recurring unavailability driving the costs.

distractors.text: Increase the sprint duration.
distractors.trap: Delays the project and makes changes costlier to absorb.

explanation: Stable velocity with rising quality costs during absences signals a skills gap; cross-training increases adaptability so members cover for each other and quality improves.

eco_task: Engage Stakeholders and Build Shared Understanding
topic: Engage Stakeholders and Build Shared Understanding
prompts: An experienced project manager is mentoring a new project manager on their first project. Overwhelmed by the number of stakeholders the team identified, the new project manager asks for guidance. What should the experienced project manager advise doing next?

correct.text: Perform a stakeholder analysis to determine relative importance.
correct.why: Analysis provides the foundational insight that categorizing and prioritizing stakeholders depend on.

distractors.text: Categorize the key stakeholders using a register workbook.
distractors.trap: Categorization should follow, not precede, the analysis.

distractors.text: Update the stakeholder engagement plan with stakeholder preferences.
distractors.trap: Planning engagement depends on analysis done first.

distractors.text: Prioritize stakeholders based on the stakeholder register.
distractors.trap: Prioritization relies on insight the analysis produces.

explanation: Once stakeholders are identified, analysis to understand each one's position and importance is foundational; categorizing, planning, and prioritizing all follow the stakeholder analysis.

eco_task: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
topic: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
prompts: During standup, the project manager learns a developer is struggling to finish work because they lack automation testing experience, threatening the sprint goal. A QA engineer with that experience is busy preparing a presentation for new employees. What action best improves the chance of meeting the sprint goal?

correct.text: Ask the QA engineer to assist the developer as soon as possible.
correct.why: Leveraging the engineer's expertise now gives timely help and keeps the sprint goal reachable.

distractors.text: Reassign the automation work to the QA engineer after the presentation is finished.
distractors.trap: Delays help and can make the developer feel their work was taken away.

distractors.text: Train the developer on automation testing immediately, then proceed with testing.
distractors.trap: Training takes time and may not yield immediate proficiency.

distractors.text: Reschedule the automation testing for when the QA engineer is available.
distractors.trap: Introduces delay that jeopardizes the sprint goal.

explanation: Timely help matters most; having the QA engineer assist the developer now leverages existing expertise to keep the sprint goal achievable, where training or rescheduling adds delay.

prompts: As a project deliverable, a project manager demonstrates a product to a group of the client's employees during a 30-minute virtual call. The employees complain that a two-day hands-on workshop would have been far more effective for their training. What should the project manager have done to prevent this complaint?

correct.text: Assessed the training needs of the client's employees.
correct.why: Understanding the audience's knowledge, complexity, and desired outcomes reveals the appropriate training format.

distractors.text: Included this risk in the risk management plan.
distractors.trap: Logging a risk documents it but does not prevent an avoidable planning gap.

distractors.text: Allocated a contingency reserve for additional training.
distractors.trap: Reserves cover unforeseen cost, not the wrong training method chosen.

distractors.text: Developed an asynchronous training plan.
distractors.trap: Assumes self-paced delivery is inherently better without knowing the need.

explanation: Assessing the employees' training needs would have revealed the required depth and format, so a suitable method could be planned. The other options do not address the root cause of the complaint.

prompts: You are managing a project being implemented simultaneously in two countries with an 11-hour time zone difference. You need to find a mutually convenient time to brief stakeholders on schedule, cost, and performance progress. Which action best addresses this?

correct.text: Use a world clock to identify a few candidate times that work for all stakeholders.
correct.why: Visualizing the time differences lets everyone attend live regardless of location.

distractors.text: Send a survey asking stakeholders for their availability during the week.
distractors.trap: Slow and unreliable; not everyone responds in time to schedule.

distractors.text: Schedule two separate meetings, one for each country.
distractors.trap: Splits the audience and risks inconsistent information across groups.

distractors.text: Record the meeting and send it for stakeholders to watch later.
distractors.trap: One-way delivery loses real-time interaction and questions.

explanation: A world clock quickly surfaces overlapping windows so all stakeholders can join a single live briefing, unlike surveys, split meetings, or recordings.

prompts: A key member of an agile team was replaced with a less experienced member. A stakeholder is concerned the project will no longer be delivered on time because of the new member. What should the project manager do to help ensure successful delivery?

correct.text: Assess the impact of the replacement and make further resource adjustments as needed.
correct.why: Understanding the actual impact lets the PM onboard, support, and adjust resources appropriately.

distractors.text: Log the concern as a risk and escalate it to the portfolio office.
distractors.trap: Escalating before assessing impact is premature.

distractors.text: Meet with the functional manager to assess the situation and get guidance.
distractors.trap: Functional manager lacks in-depth knowledge of the project's specifics.

distractors.text: Reverse the decision and bring the former key member back.
distractors.trap: Disruptive and ignores the reasons for the replacement.

explanation: The PM should first assess the impact of the change and adjust resources and onboarding accordingly, rather than escalating, deferring to a functional manager, or reversing the decision.

prompts: An agile team member is working on a user story that is overdue, causing the team to miss sprint goals for a specific feature. The reasons for the delay are unknown to the project manager. What should the project manager do first?

correct.text: Discuss the delay with the team member.
correct.why: Understanding the root cause is required before choosing an effective remedy.

distractors.text: Request additional resources from the sponsor.
distractors.trap: Adds capacity before knowing whether that is the actual problem.

distractors.text: Exclude this functionality from the final product.
distractors.trap: Dropping scope is a drastic last resort, not a first step.

distractors.text: Replace the team member with a new developer.
distractors.trap: Extreme action taken before understanding the cause.

explanation: The PM should first talk with the team member to learn the root cause of the delay before considering resources, scope cuts, or replacement.

prompts: An agile project is in the first of six iterations with a globally distributed team meeting virtually. After the fifth daily meeting, the project manager notices that not all members are actively participating. Which response is best?

correct.text: Engage and facilitate the team to encourage all members to participate.
correct.why: The PM should shape an environment where everyone is drawn into contributing.

distractors.text: Ask the team members why participation is lacking.
distractors.trap: Puts individuals on the spot rather than improving the environment.

distractors.text: Ask each member individually to participate in future meetings.
distractors.trap: Treats a team-level dynamic as isolated one-on-one requests.

distractors.text: Let the team find their own way to fully participate.
distractors.trap: Passive; a facilitative leader should actively enable participation.

explanation: A virtual team leader should actively facilitate and create conditions that encourage full participation rather than interrogating members or leaving it to chance.

prompts: In the execution phase, the project manager finds that several stakeholders identified at initiation are no longer involved. These stakeholders are frustrated by unnecessary communication and worry the remaining stakeholders may not be getting all the information they need. What should the project manager have done to prevent this situation?

correct.text: Updated the stakeholder engagement plan through the change control process.
correct.why: Managing stakeholder changes through change control keeps engagement and communications aligned.

distractors.text: Identified and documented all stakeholder needs at project start.
distractors.trap: Initial capture is done, but plans must change as stakeholders change.

distractors.text: Reported all project information to every stakeholder throughout.
distractors.trap: Engagement is tailored per stakeholder, not blanket distribution.

distractors.text: Relied on existing stakeholders to identify the missing ones.
distractors.trap: Offloads the PM's monitoring duty onto others.

explanation: When stakeholders change, the engagement plan must be updated through change control so communications match each stakeholder's current needs, which the other options fail to do.

prompts: An agile project has scaled from the feasibility phase to implementation. Due to time constraints the same team is retained, except one member who was replaced. Many team members do not get along with the new member and are unwilling to work in the next phase. How should the project manager ensure the team is ready for the next phase?

correct.text: Request a formal team commitment.
correct.why: A commitment exercise refreshes team norms and re-establishes buy-in for the new phase.

distractors.text: Perform an incremental delivery assessment.
distractors.trap: Measures output, not the interpersonal conflict blocking the team.

distractors.text: Force the team to stay together.
distractors.trap: Coercion worsens morale and does not resolve the conflict.

distractors.text: Remove the new team member.
distractors.trap: Reducing the team may leave the work undeliverable.

explanation: A formal team commitment reviews team norms and rebuilds buy-in for the new phase, addressing the interpersonal conflict that the other options ignore.

prompts: A strategic project is progressing well and the team is delivering successfully, but a key stakeholder calls the project manager to complain about not receiving progress updates. Which response is best?

correct.text: Review and update the stakeholder communications approach.
correct.why: The complaint signals the communication plan no longer meets this stakeholder's needs.

distractors.text: Make sure the stakeholder is copied on the weekly status emails.
distractors.trap: A quick fix that ignores the underlying communication gap.

distractors.text: Send the stakeholder a copy of the latest status report.
distractors.trap: One-time patch that does not ensure ongoing updates.

distractors.text: Invite the key stakeholder to attend the next project meeting.
distractors.trap: Addresses one meeting, not the structural communication need.

explanation: The complaint indicates the communications approach is inadequate for this stakeholder, so the PM should review and update it rather than apply one-off fixes.

prompts: A developer complains about time spent on daily syncs, updating stories, creating tasks, and reviewing dependencies between stories instead of writing code, and other team members agree. What should a servant leader do next?

correct.text: Acknowledge the developer's frustration and explain the benefit of each activity.
correct.why: Coaching the team on why each ceremony helps them deliver faster is a servant leader's role.

distractors.text: Ask the team for suggestions on managing their time more effectively.
distractors.trap: Deflects coaching responsibility the servant leader should provide.

distractors.text: Reduce interactions among team members to increase development time.
distractors.trap: Cutting collaboration undermines the value of agile ceremonies.

distractors.text: Explain the definition of an information radiator to the team.
distractors.trap: Irrelevant concept that does not address the frustration.

explanation: A servant leader coaches the team by acknowledging the frustration and highlighting how each activity helps them deliver, rather than cutting collaboration or deflecting.

eco_task: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
topic: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
prompts: You lead a project team whose goal is to enhance a product's capability in the market. What should be the top consideration when defining the team's key performance indicators (KPIs)?

correct.text: Alignment with the customer's needs
correct.why: The project's aim is to benefit the customer, so KPIs should measure customer value first.

distractors.text: Alignment with an individual's growth and development
distractors.trap: A secondary consideration, not the primary project outcome.

distractors.text: Alignment with the project manager's goals
distractors.trap: The PM's goals are not the ultimate measure of team performance.

distractors.text: Alignment with the sponsor's business goals
distractors.trap: Important but subordinate to the customer benefit being delivered.

explanation: Since the project exists to enhance the product's value for the customer, KPIs should primarily align with customer needs, with individual, sponsor, and team goals secondary.

eco_task: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
topic: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
prompts: A new warehouse facility is under construction. The project manager implements team development efforts such as training that would result in a lower defect ratio. What is the most appropriate next step?

correct.text: Update the resource management plan.
correct.why: Team development changes should be reflected in the resource management plan.

distractors.text: Introduce a training program.
distractors.trap: The training initiative already exists; this repeats the action.

distractors.text: Increase quality control reviews.
distractors.trap: Adds inspection instead of documenting the development effort.

distractors.text: Add training costs to the budget.
distractors.trap: Focuses on one cost detail, not updating the governing plan.

explanation: After implementing team development such as training, the PM should update the resource management plan to reflect the change.

prompts: During a review meeting, the project manager detects a misunderstanding about one critical deliverable. Which action should come first?

correct.text: Ask the team members to clarify the deliverable.
correct.why: The team building the deliverable is best positioned to clarify it first.

distractors.text: Ask the customer to clarify the deliverable.
distractors.trap: Escalates outward before the team resolves it internally.

distractors.text: Ask the product owner to clarify the deliverable.
distractors.trap: Bypasses the team who should first reach shared understanding.

distractors.text: Ask the sponsor to clarify the deliverable.
distractors.trap: The sponsor is a last resort, not the first point of clarification.

explanation: The team producing the deliverable should first reach a shared understanding; escalation to the customer, product owner, or sponsor comes only if they cannot.

eco_task: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
topic: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
prompts: An agile project is midway through its ten iterations. In the last iteration the team worked overtime to meet its goal, and the retrospective concluded they overran because they lacked experience the first time they performed the assigned tasks. What should the project manager do next?

correct.text: Have the team re-assess their effort estimates in the next iteration planning session.
correct.why: The team now has experience, so re-estimating during planning confirms the work fits the timebox.

distractors.text: Ask the executive sponsor to arrange additional team training.
distractors.trap: Assumes a skills gap remains after the team has already gained the experience.

distractors.text: Ask the sponsor to authorize more time for future iterations.
distractors.trap: Extends the schedule before checking whether the team can now finish on time.

distractors.text: Ask the product owner to reprioritize the product backlog.
distractors.trap: Reprioritizing backlog value does not address the team's estimating accuracy.

explanation: Retrospective actions are applied in the next planning session; because the team has now learned the work, re-assessing their estimates confirms they can deliver within the timebox without extra time or training.

prompts: A project manager is assigned to a new project with several senior stakeholders. Each stakeholder has stated their expectations, and the project manager notices that some of these expectations conflict with one another. What is the best response?

correct.text: Facilitate a meeting to surface and resolve the conflicting expectations.
correct.why: Neutral facilitation lets stakeholders align collaboratively, preventing later confusion and dissension.

distractors.text: Prioritize the expectations of the stakeholders with the highest authority.
distractors.trap: Authority ranking ignores the conflict and fails when peers disagree.

distractors.text: Update the stakeholder register with each stakeholder's expectations.
distractors.trap: Documenting expectations records the conflict but does not resolve it.

distractors.text: Create a project hierarchy to manage the stakeholders' expectations.
distractors.trap: A hierarchy structures reporting but leaves the substantive conflict unaddressed.

explanation: When stakeholders hold conflicting expectations, the project manager should collaborate through neutral facilitation to understand needs and resolve the conflict, rather than ranking by authority or merely recording the expectations.

prompts: A team is delivering a regulatory application with a fixed deadline using a hybrid approach across three sprints. The first two sprints missed their targets, largely due to delays from functional units. The finance director is anxious about looming sanctions and the team is demoralized. Who is responsible for motivating the project team?

correct.text: Project manager
correct.why: Inspiring and motivating the team is a core leadership responsibility of the project manager.

distractors.text: Product owner
distractors.trap: The product owner manages backlog value, not team morale and leadership.

distractors.text: Finance director
distractors.trap: The finance director focuses on budget and finances, not team motivation.

distractors.text: Functional manager
distractors.trap: The functional manager supplies resources but does not lead the project team.

explanation: Motivating and inspiring the team is a leadership duty of the project manager; the finance director handles budget and the functional manager provides resources.

prompts: You are leading a complex application build. The project team cannot agree on which process to use to solve a specific problem. What approach should you take to help the team reach consensus?

correct.text: Facilitate a meeting and encourage the specialists to propose potential solutions.
correct.why: Collaborative facilitation drives creative problem solving and team engagement toward consensus.

distractors.text: Exert your expert authority and define the path forward for the team.
distractors.trap: Imposing a decision undermines engagement and self-organization.

distractors.text: Update the RACI matrix and reassign the work to a different team.
distractors.trap: Reassigning avoids the disagreement instead of resolving it.

distractors.text: Assign a new team lead experienced in similar software problems.
distractors.trap: Bringing in a lead sidelines the team rather than building its consensus.

explanation: A collaborative, facilitated approach that lets the specialists generate solutions fosters engagement and creative problem solving, which is more effective than imposing authority or reassigning the work.

prompts: For a team to function well, its project charter should state the project's vision or purpose along with clear working agreements. Which question should the project charter primarily answer?

correct.text: Why is this project being done?
correct.why: The charter's purpose is to convey the project's vision and reason for existing.

distractors.text: Why did the organization prioritize this project?
distractors.trap: Portfolio selection rationale is not the charter's statement of purpose.

distractors.text: What are the key risks and their responses?
distractors.trap: Risk detail belongs in the risk register, not the charter's vision.

distractors.text: Who will be doing the work?
distractors.trap: Staffing is a planning concern, not the charter's vision or purpose.

explanation: The charter conveys the project's vision and purpose, so it should answer why the project is being done; risks, staffing, and prioritization rationale are addressed elsewhere.

prompts: An agile project is in the first of ten iterations. At the iteration review, stakeholders report that the first deliverable is not aligned with the project objectives. What is the best response?

correct.text: Ask the product owner to review the product backlog and its priorities.
correct.why: The product owner owns backlog priorities, so realigning it addresses the misalignment first.

distractors.text: Have the team meet with stakeholders to understand their needs.
distractors.trap: Useful, but not the first step before the backlog is realigned.

distractors.text: Ask stakeholders to prioritize their needs.
distractors.trap: Stakeholder prioritization feeds the backlog rather than replacing its review.

distractors.text: Ask the sponsor to define the stakeholders' priorities.
distractors.trap: Priorities flow through the product owner, not the sponsor.

explanation: The product owner is responsible for the product backlog and its priorities, so when a deliverable misaligns with objectives, the first step is to have them review and realign the backlog.

prompts: During execution, a project manager learns that a team member's functional manager wants to pull them off the project, believing they should not perform a certain task because of competing priorities. Which action should come first?

correct.text: Check the responsibility assignment matrix (RAM).
correct.why: The RAM confirms who is responsible for the task before addressing the dispute.

distractors.text: Check the work breakdown structure (WBS).
distractors.trap: The WBS decomposes scope but does not assign task responsibility.

distractors.text: Check the organizational chart.
distractors.trap: The org chart shows reporting lines, not task-level responsibility.

distractors.text: Check the resource management plan.
distractors.trap: The resource plan lists resources but not their specific task assignments.

explanation: The RAM identifies who is responsible for each task, so the project manager should verify the team member's responsibility there before discussing the concern with the functional manager.

prompts: Over a one-year project, the team delivered everything the product owner defined, met every sprint goal, and stayed on schedule, scope, and budget. Months after release, satisfaction surveys show surprisingly low customer satisfaction. What should the project manager do to prevent this in the future?

correct.text: Conduct periodic reviews with the customer involved.
correct.why: Ongoing customer involvement ensures the team builds the right product, not just a well-run one.

distractors.text: Hold a kick-off meeting at the start of the project.
distractors.trap: A single early meeting does not provide continuous customer feedback.

distractors.text: Improve the application of test automation techniques.
distractors.trap: Automation improves quality of build, not fit to customer needs.

distractors.text: Increase the PMO's involvement in the project.
distractors.trap: More PMO oversight does not address missing customer input.

explanation: The team executed well but lacked real customer input, so they built the wrong product; periodic reviews with the customer keep the work aligned to actual needs and satisfaction.

prompts: While preparing the charter for a new project, a project manager reviews lessons learned and finds recurring problems with low engagement and stakeholder misalignment on objectives. What should the project manager do to avoid repeating this?

correct.text: Build a shared understanding among stakeholders of the key deliverables in the approved charter.
correct.why: Common understanding of the charter's deliverables directly addresses misalignment on objectives.

distractors.text: Allow any stakeholder to join the governance board at any point.
distractors.trap: Open-ended board access invites over-involvement, not alignment.

distractors.text: Include all stakeholders in the RAM for deliverable acceptance.
distractors.trap: Adding everyone to acceptance dilutes accountability without resolving misalignment.

distractors.text: Collect every stakeholder's needs and add them all to the WBS.
distractors.trap: Loading all needs into the WBS creates scope bloat, not alignment.

explanation: Stakeholder misalignment is best prevented by building a common understanding of the key deliverables described in the approved charter; broadening board access, acceptance roles, or the WBS only adds unnecessary involvement.

prompts: A highly skilled SME is engaged on a government project where part of the work follows an agile approach. Invited to join the agile team, the SME is reluctant, believing teamwork is demotivating and slows them down, and they want the highest-quality output. What should the project manager do to address these concerns and encourage the SME to join?

correct.text: Explain that teamwork fosters continuous improvement and early feedback loops.
correct.why: It directly addresses the quality concern by linking collaboration to higher-quality output.

distractors.text: Suggest the SME build emotional intelligence by integrating with the team.
distractors.trap: Presumes the SME lacks emotional intelligence and ignores their concern.

distractors.text: Ask the SME to run the retrospectives as an objective observer.
distractors.trap: Sidesteps the concern and puts the SME in an uncomfortable role.

distractors.text: Ask the sponsor to meet with the SME to improve their attitude.
distractors.trap: Escalating to the sponsor is premature before the PM tries directly.

explanation: The project manager should directly address the SME's quality concern by explaining that agile teamwork's collaboration and feedback loops produce higher-quality output, rather than deflecting or escalating.

eco_task: Engage Stakeholders and Build Shared Understanding
topic: Engage Stakeholders and Build Shared Understanding
prompts: During a kick-off meeting with the executive sponsor and various project members, it became clear that several attendees did not need to be there. Meanwhile, a required subject-matter expert was unaware of the meeting and did not attend. What should the project manager have done to prevent this?

correct.text: Managed stakeholder engagement by involving each stakeholder in the phases suitable to them.
correct.why: Active engagement management clarifies when each stakeholder's participation is required or optional.

distractors.text: Established a fixed communication platform to provide project updates to all stakeholders.
distractors.trap: A broadcast channel informs but does not tailor who attends which events.

distractors.text: Developed a project charter listing the roles and responsibilities of all stakeholders.
distractors.trap: Roles in a charter do not manage ongoing attendance expectations.

distractors.text: Created a responsibility matrix showing which meetings each stakeholder must attend.
distractors.trap: A static matrix lists duties but does not actively engage or notify stakeholders.

explanation: Actively managing stakeholder engagement ensures the right stakeholders are involved at the right times, so required experts know when their participation is needed and others are not over-invited.

eco_task: Promote Team Performance Through the Application of Emotional Intelligence
topic: Promote Team Performance Through the Application of Emotional Intelligence
prompts: During a standup, several delivery specialists strongly disagreed about how tasks are distributed. They are tired and frustrated because task distribution has been a recurring complaint throughout the project. What is the best response?

correct.text: Partner with the team to develop a plan for addressing the task distribution concerns.
correct.why: Working with the team targets the actual grievance and produces a fair, agreed resolution.

distractors.text: Review the project management plan to determine how to manage the situation.
distractors.trap: General guidelines don't resolve the specific fairness concerns the team raised.

distractors.text: Discuss with other project managers whether they faced similar complaints.
distractors.trap: Seeking peer input delays action and does not resolve the team's issue.

distractors.text: Organize a team-building workshop to improve collaboration and communication.
distractors.trap: The problem is perceived unfair workload, not weak collaboration or communication.

explanation: The frustration stems from perceived unfair workload, so the PM should collaborate with the team on a fair plan to address task distribution rather than treat it as a communication problem.

prompts: A team using agile practices is developing an application. A senior delivery specialist attends team meetings inconsistently, including the daily syncs, and asks other specialists to give status updates on their behalf. What should the project manager do to handle this?

correct.text: Remind the senior team member to adhere to the team's working agreement.
correct.why: The PM should first address the behavior directly against the agreed ground rules.

distractors.text: Request removal of the senior team member for the participation issues.
distractors.trap: Removal is a last-resort escalation before attempting to resolve it directly.

distractors.text: Escalate the senior member's participation to upper management.
distractors.trap: Escalating first skips the PM's own attempt to resolve the issue.

distractors.text: Ask the other delivery specialists to cover the senior member's tasks.
distractors.trap: Covering for absence is unsustainable and harms team dynamics.

explanation: The PM should first resolve the issue directly by reminding the member of the team's working agreement; escalation or removal comes only if direct resolution fails.

eco_task: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
topic: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
prompts: An inexperienced project manager needs HR support. The HR manager hired interns and sent them for training, but the training failed to deliver the competency-based outcomes. The HR manager is unwilling to absorb the cost because the training budget is exhausted. What should the project manager do to resolve the situation?

correct.text: Identify the competency gaps and work with the HR manager on options.
correct.why: Collaborating on the actual gaps moves toward a workable solution for the current problem.

distractors.text: Prepare clear training needs for full-time staff and interns.
distractors.trap: Defining needs is useful but does not resolve the present budget and gap issue.

distractors.text: Communicate the training and hiring needs to the HR manager.
distractors.trap: Restating needs does not address the failed outcomes or exhausted budget.

distractors.text: Analyze the risk, update the risk register, and inform the HR manager.
distractors.trap: Documenting risk records the problem instead of solving it.

explanation: The other options record or restate needs; the effective resolution is to work with the HR manager to pinpoint the competency gaps and identify solution options together.

prompts: During a weekly team meeting, the project manager finds that a senior team member did not follow the plan, instead performing tasks by personal preference without communicating with the project manager. This member is an expert and a critical resource. As project manager, how should you handle this?

correct.text: Discuss it with the resource personally, then update the team charter if necessary.
correct.why: Address the person directly first, then adjust the team's agreements if warranted.

distractors.text: Discuss it with the resource personally, then update the resource management plan if necessary.
distractors.trap: Ground rules for behavior belong in the team charter, not the resource plan.

distractors.text: Discuss it with the functional manager, then update the resource management plan if necessary.
distractors.trap: Going to the functional manager first escalates before talking to the person.

distractors.text: Discuss it with the functional manager, then update the team charter if necessary.
distractors.trap: Bypasses the direct conversation the PM should have first.

explanation: The PM should first talk to the resource directly to understand the reason and resolve it, updating the team charter (which holds team values and operating agreements) if needed, rather than escalating to the functional manager.

prompts: A company is running a hybrid project in which the deliverables have quality issues at the end of one of the sprints. Which action should the project manager take?

correct.text: Review the definition of done (DoD).
correct.why: The DoD sets the completion criteria used to pinpoint and correct the quality gaps.

distractors.text: Look for a clear set of working agreements.
distractors.trap: Working agreements manage behavior, not deliverable quality criteria.

distractors.text: Apply value stream mapping.
distractors.trap: That targets process waste, not the specific quality issues in the output.

distractors.text: Adopt a plan-driven approach.
distractors.trap: Switching methods is less flexible and doesn't directly fix the quality issues.

explanation: The definition of done is the agreed set of criteria a deliverable must meet before it is complete, so reviewing it lets the PM identify the quality gaps and plan the fixes.

eco_task: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
topic: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
prompts: An agile team is doing an excellent job focusing on high-priority items and completing them in less time than required. Which key performance indicator would best demonstrate this to the project manager?

correct.text: The number of backlog items completed per iteration.
correct.why: It directly shows how many prioritized items the team delivers each iteration.

distractors.text: The number of defects or missed requirements flagged by the value representative.
distractors.trap: Measures deliverable quality, not throughput of prioritized work.

distractors.text: The number of action items from the end-of-iteration retrospective.
distractors.trap: Reflects process improvement, not completion of high-priority items.

distractors.text: Whether the team consistently stays within the kanban work-in-process limits.
distractors.trap: Shows workflow control, not the volume of prioritized items delivered.

explanation: Backlog items completed per iteration measures how many high-priority items the team delivers each cycle, best demonstrating its focus and speed on prioritized work.

prompts: A new project manager is assigned to an ongoing agile project entering its second four-week iteration. The PM finds significant differences of opinion within the team that are causing major setbacks. What should the project manager do to help overcome the differences and get the project back on track?

correct.text: Facilitate discussion among the delivery specialists in upcoming review meetings to solve the problem.
correct.why: A servant leader proactively promotes collaboration and dialogue to resolve conflict.

distractors.text: Schedule a team outing so people get to know each other and feel comfortable talking.
distractors.trap: Social bonding doesn't directly resolve the substantive disagreements.

distractors.text: Let the team self-organize before the next iteration to solve the problem.
distractors.trap: Stepping back leaves the PM disengaged from active conflict resolution.

distractors.text: Ask the functional managers to talk to the delivery specialists and resolve it.
distractors.trap: Delegating conflict resolution shows the PM is not proactively involved.

explanation: Conflict is a natural part of team interaction, and a servant-leader PM should proactively facilitate collaboration and dialogue among the team to resolve it rather than delegating or stepping away.

prompts: An agile project to implement a new factory automation process is underway. The project manager needs a vendor to help with the installation. What should the project manager do before the vendor comes to work with the team?

correct.text: Hold an orientation so both parties share the same understanding of project goals and needs.
correct.why: Aligning the vendor on goals, expectations, and approach up front prevents future impasses.

distractors.text: Delegate all key decisions to the vendor's team so the PM avoids taking risks.
distractors.trap: Abdicating decisions is situational and cedes control the PM should keep.

distractors.text: Send the communication matrix so all specialists and vendors are identified.
distractors.trap: Sharing contacts is not always needed and is less vital than aligning understanding.

distractors.text: Remind the team that the vendor works for them and the team makes final decisions.
distractors.trap: Framing authority does not build shared understanding of the work.

explanation: Integrating a key partner starts with an orientation or kick-off where both parties align on goals, expectations, responsibilities, and approach, avoiding future issues or impasses.

prompts: A critical application build has been in development for six sprints. During a demonstration two days before the final sprint, a new stakeholder states the product will not meet market expectations. What is the most appropriate next step?

correct.text: Review the project scope and plan with the new stakeholder.
correct.why: Clarifying scope with the stakeholder verifies the concern before any change is made.

distractors.text: Ask the executive sponsor and new stakeholder to resolve the expectation issue.
distractors.trap: Delegating the clarification skips the PM's own review of the concern.

distractors.text: Review the list of required tasks with the development team and new stakeholder.
distractors.trap: Task lists don't clarify whether scope actually misses expectations.

distractors.text: Follow the change management plan and revise scope and plan on the stakeholder's request.
distractors.trap: Assumes the stakeholder is right and changes scope without verifying.

explanation: The PM should not assume the new stakeholder is correct; the first step is to review the scope and plan with them to clarify and validate the concern before considering any change.

prompts: A multinational company is locally developing a digital product on a new technology platform. The only platform expert is at headquarters and supports multiple countries. How can the local project manager ensure this expert's involvement?

correct.text: Include the expert on the team through a virtual environment.
correct.why: A virtual environment engages the expert as a team member while preserving their other duties.

distractors.text: Arrange a travel schedule so timely onsite assistance can be provided.
distractors.trap: Travel consumes time from both the project and the expert's other duties.

distractors.text: Ask the executive sponsor to assign the expert full-time to the team.
distractors.trap: The expert can't easily be reassigned given other support responsibilities.

distractors.text: Have specialists contact the expert as an external source on an as-needed basis.
distractors.trap: Occasional consultation is weaker than engaging them as a team member.

explanation: The expert is remote and has ongoing responsibilities, so engaging them virtually as a full team member—rather than reassigning, flying them in, or only consulting them—best ensures their involvement.

prompts: A pilot project using an agile approach is delivering the planned increments on time, but top management is concerned because they do not see the value the project is adding. As project manager, how should you communicate the project's value to top management?

correct.text: Demonstrate how the project's objectives align with the organization's strategic goals.
correct.why: Linking outcomes to strategy is the clearest way to convey business value to leadership.

distractors.text: Include top management in all future sprint reviews to address their concerns live.
distractors.trap: Attendance may be impractical and doesn't clearly convey strategic value.

distractors.text: Send weekly status reports and invite them to retrospectives.
distractors.trap: Retrospectives focus on process; status reports show progress, not value.

distractors.text: Tailor progress reports to top management's needs.
distractors.trap: Progress data shows activity, not the project's strategic contribution.

explanation: Top management sees value in strategic terms, so demonstrating how the project's objectives align with the organization's strategic goals is the most effective way to communicate its value.

eco_task: Promote Team Performance Through the Application of Emotional Intelligence
topic: Promote Team Performance Through the Application of Emotional Intelligence
prompts: As project manager, you notice that a team member is becoming increasingly disengaged from an ongoing project. What should the project manager do to help ensure this member's participation?

correct.text: Speak with the team member, understand the problem, and agree on corrective actions.
correct.why: Understanding the cause first, with empathy, is essential before acting.

distractors.text: Ask another team member to mentor the disengaged team member.
distractors.trap: Assigns a fix before understanding what is actually wrong.

distractors.text: Call a team meeting to discuss engagement expectations.
distractors.trap: Addresses the whole team without diagnosing this individual's issue.

distractors.text: Ask the team member to take additional training to improve communication skills.
distractors.trap: Assumes a skills gap before understanding the real cause of disengagement.

explanation: The PM should first speak with the team member to understand the underlying problem, showing empathy, and then agree on corrective actions rather than acting before diagnosing the cause.

prompts: Shortly after a sprint starts, a key stakeholder emails the team expressing displeasure with the work that just began, despite being inexperienced in agile. The stakeholder insists on changing the internal and external deliverables. What should the project manager do next?

correct.text: Facilitate a meeting with the value representative and the stakeholder to align on deliverables.
correct.why: The value representative owns backlog alignment and should reconcile expectations with the stakeholder.

distractors.text: Ask the team for their assessment of the matter at the next standup.
distractors.trap: Standups are for progress, not resolving stakeholder expectation disputes.

distractors.text: Change the backlog and comply with the stakeholder's request.
distractors.trap: Acts prematurely before aligning on the actual deliverables.

distractors.text: Encourage the stakeholder to join a discussion on backlog clarification.
distractors.trap: Involves only the stakeholder, omitting the value representative who owns alignment.

explanation: The value representative is responsible for backlog and deliverable alignment, so the PM should facilitate a meeting between them and the key stakeholder to align on deliverables before any change.

eco_task: Engage Stakeholders and Build Shared Understanding
topic: Engage Stakeholders and Build Shared Understanding
prompts: While a project is being executed, its project manager is reassigned. A new project manager takes over and quickly realizes the project has quality and scheduling issues with some deliverables. What should the new project manager do first?

correct.text: Meet with the project team to assess the situation and develop a plan to address the issues.
correct.why: The team knows the issues best, so the PM should understand them before acting.

distractors.text: Update the project plan to reflect new schedule and quality requirements.
distractors.trap: Changes the plan before understanding the actual root cause.

distractors.text: Review the schedule and focus on delivering on time and within budget.
distractors.trap: Prioritizing dates does not address the quality and scheduling root causes.

distractors.text: Assess the team's skills and ask the sponsor for additional resources.
distractors.trap: Adding resources presumes a cause before the situation is assessed.

explanation: The new PM should not make decisions without understanding the problems; meeting with the team, who know the issues best, to assess and plan is the correct first step.

eco_task: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
topic: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
prompts: A newly launched product prototype is receiving many customer complaints. To reduce negative feedback, the team creates an action plan. By quarter-end, however, the project manager finds no significant reduction in complaints. What is the most appropriate next step?

correct.text: Revisit and reevaluate the project's key performance indicators (KPIs).
correct.why: Reassessing KPIs checks whether the action plan is effective and realigns the team's goals.

distractors.text: Identify the top performers on the team and recognize them appropriately.
distractors.trap: Recognition doesn't address the persistent customer complaints.

distractors.text: Identify delivery specialists who lack the required skills and train them.
distractors.trap: No evidence points to a skills gap causing the complaints.

distractors.text: Report the status to executive management and seek help.
distractors.trap: Escalation is a last resort; agile teams first own problem-solving.

explanation: In agile, KPIs should be revisited frequently to gauge whether the action plan is working; since complaints have not dropped, reevaluating the KPIs lets the team quickly identify and address what is preventing progress.

prompts: As project manager, you have created the stakeholder list and are now deciding how to distribute information effectively to the customer. What is the most appropriate next step?

correct.text: Perform a stakeholder analysis and prepare a stakeholder communications approach.
correct.why: Analysis identifies each stakeholder's communication needs so the right approach can be planned.

distractors.text: Meet with all stakeholders face-to-face on a weekly basis.
distractors.trap: Not always practical, and not the right method or frequency for every stakeholder.

distractors.text: Send updates to all stakeholders, including the customer.
distractors.trap: Broadcasting ignores individual communication needs and preferences.

distractors.text: Review historical data sent to other customers and follow the same practices.
distractors.trap: Past practices still require identifying this project's specific needs.

explanation: The PM should perform stakeholder analysis to identify each stakeholder's communication needs and preferences, then build the communications approach on that basis rather than broadcasting or copying past practice.

prompts: As project manager, you have been assigned a project with a virtual, multicultural team of representatives from various countries, and you need to prepare a kickoff meeting. What should the project manager do to prepare for it?

correct.text: Consider the various time zones, communication capabilities, and possible constraints.
correct.why: Accounting for team culture, availability, and preferences enables an inclusive kickoff.

distractors.text: Set the rules and regulations that must be followed during the project.
distractors.trap: Imposing rules ignores the team's cultural and logistical realities.

distractors.text: Ask the sponsor for different resources to facilitate the project.
distractors.trap: Requesting resources doesn't prepare an inclusive kickoff meeting.

distractors.text: Set the executive sponsor's language as the official project language.
distractors.trap: Mandating one language dismisses the team's multicultural needs.

explanation: For a geographically dispersed, multicultural virtual team, the PM must account for enterprise environmental factors such as time zones, communication capabilities, availability, and constraints when preparing the kickoff.

prompts: As project manager in a rapidly growing hybrid organization, you are increasingly frustrated by the pace of change in daily operations, and several newer delivery specialists have already left the team or moved to other roles. What should the project manager do to keep project governance stable?

correct.text: Partner with leadership to update the organizational process assets (OPAs).
correct.why: Updating OPAs gives the organization a consistent, clear basis for how work is governed.

distractors.text: Create a new social contract to empower the team.
distractors.trap: A team agreement helps morale but doesn't stabilize organization-wide governance.

distractors.text: Revise the team charter with the newer delivery specialists.
distractors.trap: Charter updates aid the team but don't secure organizational governance.

distractors.text: Revise the project management plan on a regular basis.
distractors.trap: Frequent plan updates manage the project, not enterprise governance stability.

explanation: Working with leadership to update the organizational process assets gives the organization a clear, consistent basis for how work should be done, stabilizing project governance amid rapid change.

eco_task: Promote Team Performance Through the Application of Emotional Intelligence
topic: Promote Team Performance Through the Application of Emotional Intelligence
prompts: As a project manager who previously worked on predictive projects, you have started leading a project that uses a hybrid approach. What should the project manager do to enable the team to focus on their mission?

correct.text: Adopt servant leadership.
correct.why: Serving the team's needs and development enables the highest possible team performance.

distractors.text: Implement a Scrum method.
distractors.trap: Prescribing a framework doesn't itself enable the team to focus on its mission.

distractors.text: Apply lean methods.
distractors.trap: A process technique that doesn't directly ease the predictive-to-hybrid transition.

distractors.text: Innovate with new processes.
distractors.trap: New processes add change rather than creating a supportive focus environment.

explanation: Servant leadership focuses on understanding and meeting the team's needs and development to unlock the highest performance, best enabling the team to focus on its mission during the transition to hybrid.

prompts: During a retrospective, several delivery team members say they strongly disagree with how the product owner and agile coach handled scope issues in the last iteration. Which action should the project manager take first?

correct.text: Gather all available information from the team before addressing the problem.
correct.why: You must understand the conflict's root cause and full context before shaping any resolution approach.

distractors.text: Review the backlog with the product owner to confirm the scope is appropriate.
distractors.trap: Jumps to a scope fix before understanding the interpersonal conflict.

distractors.text: Schedule a meeting with only the specific team members to discuss the issue.
distractors.trap: Excludes parties to the conflict instead of engaging the whole team.

distractors.text: Raise the team's concerns directly with the product owner and agile coach.
distractors.trap: Acts on one side's account before gathering the full picture.

explanation: When conflict surfaces, the project manager first collects all available information from the team to understand the root cause, then develops a situational approach in a safe, respectful environment.

prompts: A new project manager takes over a project in execution and finds that team performance has dropped sharply, hurting the KPIs. The same team performed well on similar projects before. What should the project manager do next?

correct.text: Hold a team meeting to identify the root cause of the declining performance.
correct.why: You cannot fix a performance drop without first understanding what changed for a previously high-performing team.

distractors.text: Review the resource management plan and organizational process assets.
distractors.trap: Looks for causes in documents instead of the team itself.

distractors.text: Apply schedule compression techniques to bring the project back on track.
distractors.trap: Treats a symptom without diagnosing the underlying cause.

distractors.text: Estimate the baseline impacts and submit a change request.
distractors.trap: Escalates to change control before the problem is even understood.

explanation: A previously strong team's sudden decline signals an unaddressed root cause, often the leadership transition itself. The project manager must diagnose it before acting.

prompts: An organization has recently adopted agile. The executive sponsor is frustrated and does not understand the new requirements-gathering process, which seems incomplete and too fast to them. How should the project manager respond?

correct.text: Help the sponsor understand the available tools and techniques and why this one was chosen.
correct.why: Agile practitioners mentor stakeholders so they understand the process and can participate effectively.

distractors.text: Update the stakeholder register to flag the sponsor as a potential impediment.
distractors.trap: Labels a key stakeholder an obstacle, breaking the trust model.

distractors.text: Tell the team the sponsor is unhappy and switch from iterative to predictive planning.
distractors.trap: Abandons agile under pressure instead of educating the sponsor.

distractors.text: Pause the work until the agile coach reviews the completed backlog with the sponsor.
distractors.trap: Implies the whole backlog must be pre-authorized, reverting to waterfall.

explanation: A sponsor is a partner, not an impediment. The project manager mentors the sponsor on the process so they can engage effectively within an agile approach.

prompts: A senior team member is struggling to work with a new team member and asks the project manager for advice on how to handle it. How should the project manager respond?

correct.text: Coach the senior member on conflict resolution models and how to apply them here.
correct.why: Coaching empowers the member to resolve the conflict directly and builds open communication skills.

distractors.text: Review the member's performance and the responsibility assignment matrix.
distractors.trap: Assumes a performance problem when the issue is interpersonal.

distractors.text: Tell the senior member to confront the new member at the next daily standup.
distractors.trap: Public confrontation escalates conflict rather than resolving it.

distractors.text: Meet with the new member one-on-one and tell them to avoid the senior member.
distractors.trap: Avoidance leaves the conflict unresolved and harms the team.

explanation: Nothing indicates a performance problem. The project manager coaches the senior member on conflict resolution, empowering them to handle the situation constructively.

prompts: On an internal project, the project manager notices that other department leaders' visions and goals are unclear, creating confusion and making it hard to separate wish-list items from actual scope. Which document should the project manager review to clarify this?

correct.text: Project charter
correct.why: The charter records the project's authorized purpose, vision, and high-level objectives aligned to business strategy.

distractors.text: Stakeholder communications approach
distractors.trap: Governs how to communicate, not the project's vision and goals.

distractors.text: Risk register
distractors.trap: Tracks threats and opportunities, not project purpose or scope intent.

distractors.text: Stakeholder engagement plan
distractors.trap: Manages engagement levels, not the authorized project vision.

explanation: The project charter documents why the project exists and its high-level goals, making it the source for clarifying an unclear vision and distinguishing scope from wish-list items.

prompts: A senior manager tells the project manager that the project is not performing as well as others. What should the project manager use to improve performance over the next several iterations?

correct.text: Retrospective session
correct.why: The retrospective reviews progress against the plan and identifies concrete changes to improve upcoming iterations.

distractors.text: Backlog analysis
distractors.trap: Only surfaces work for the next iteration, not performance improvements.

distractors.text: Team building
distractors.trap: Helpful generally, but doesn't first diagnose actual performance gaps.

distractors.text: Team motivation
distractors.trap: Addresses morale without first understanding what's underperforming.

explanation: A retrospective examines progress and pinpoints needed changes, giving the project manager and manager the understanding required to improve the next iterations.

prompts: On an agile project with an experienced team, the project manager occasionally sits in on daily standups. After one standup, the project manager notices it was unproductive. What should the project manager do to improve the situation?

correct.text: Analyze the meeting dynamics, remove impediments, and provide feedback.
correct.why: Servant leadership means understanding the root cause and clearing obstacles while preserving the team's self-organization.

distractors.text: Ask the team to send meeting minutes after each daily standup.
distractors.trap: Adds status-reporting overhead that undermines standup purpose.

distractors.text: Start attending every daily standup to improve collaboration.
distractors.trap: Presence can turn the standup into a status meeting, eroding autonomy.

distractors.text: Delegate facilitation of future standups to the product owner.
distractors.trap: Shifts ownership away from the team that should self-organize.

explanation: As a servant leader, the project manager first understands why the standup faltered, removes impediments, and offers feedback, without undermining the team's autonomy and self-organization.

eco_task: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
topic: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
prompts: During initiation of a new project, the project manager is preparing a team charter. The team is cross-departmental, and collaboration is critical to success. What should the project manager do to create an effective team charter?

correct.text: Hold a kick-off meeting to collaboratively define the team's purpose, roles, and objectives.
correct.why: Co-creating the charter builds shared ownership and clarifies each member's responsibilities up front.

distractors.text: Draft the team charter independently, outlining expectations and goals for the team.
distractors.trap: Solo authoring skips buy-in and creates no shared ownership.

distractors.text: Delegate drafting the charter to an experienced senior team member.
distractors.trap: Delegation excludes the wider team from defining their norms.

distractors.text: Share a generic charter template and invite the team to modify it as needed.
distractors.trap: Editing a template doesn't fully engage members in defining roles.

explanation: A team charter captures the team's values, agreements, and norms. Co-creating it in a kick-off meeting ensures cross-departmental input, ownership, and clear roles.

prompts: A project manager notices conflicts between team members in every sprint and, after investigating, concludes the team needs training in agile techniques to handle them. Which action should come first?

correct.text: Conduct a training needs assessment for the team.
correct.why: Assessing needs first ensures the training targets the specific gaps driving the conflicts.

distractors.text: Create a training plan for the team.
distractors.trap: Planning before assessment risks training on irrelevant techniques.

distractors.text: Schedule training sessions for the team.
distractors.trap: Scheduling without a plan produces haphazard, untargeted training.

distractors.text: Instruct the team to start using agile techniques immediately.
distractors.trap: Directs practice before the team has been trained on the techniques.

explanation: A training needs assessment identifies exactly which agile techniques the team lacks, ensuring the training is tailored and actually addresses the root cause of the conflicts.

prompts: A key stakeholder was engaged early in a complex agile project but grew less involved as sprints progressed due to other duties. Several sprints later, the stakeholder rejected a delivered feature, and the team must rework it. What should the project manager have done to prevent this?

correct.text: Involved the key stakeholder in the decision-making process.
correct.why: Ongoing involvement keeps stakeholder expectations understood and reduces rejections of delivered work.

distractors.text: Documented the project vision and objectives.
distractors.trap: Documentation alone doesn't keep a disengaging stakeholder involved.

distractors.text: Analyzed the changes in stakeholder attributes.
distractors.trap: Noting the change without re-engaging the stakeholder solves nothing.

distractors.text: Tailored stakeholder communications to their needs.
distractors.trap: Better messaging can't substitute for active participation in decisions.

explanation: Keeping the key stakeholder in the decision-making process ensures their expectations are understood and applied, preventing rejection of features they were never consulted on.

prompts: An agile project is in the first of 12 two-week iterations. At the first iteration review, stakeholders ask to receive monthly reports going forward. How should the project manager respond?

correct.text: Coach and mentor the stakeholders on agile methodologies.
correct.why: Monthly reporting misaligns with two-week feedback loops; educating stakeholders keeps them engaged the agile way.

distractors.text: Tell the delivery team to create monthly iteration reports.
distractors.trap: Imposes reporting overhead that conflicts with the cadence.

distractors.text: Create the monthly reports yourself and send them to stakeholders.
distractors.trap: Accommodates a request that undermines agile transparency.

distractors.text: Ask the stakeholders to attend monthly iteration reviews.
distractors.trap: Monthly reviews skip the two-week feedback the iterations require.
