distractors.trap: Escalation neither builds team capability nor meets commitments.

distractors.text: Inform management that commitments will not be met.
distractors.trap: Reports the problem rather than helping the team improve.

distractors.text: Raise the issues in the retrospective and propose solutions.
distractors.trap: Waiting for the retrospective is too late for an urgent struggle.

explanation: A newly forming team benefits most from coaching at both the team and individual levels, which improves performance and addresses the missed commitments and conflicts together.

eco_task: Engage Stakeholders and Build Shared Understanding
topic: Engage Stakeholders and Build Shared Understanding
prompts: A gas pipeline infrastructure upgrade was planned for 36 months. After 24 months, local government authorities suspended it, citing a breach of social impact procedures. What should the project manager have done to avoid this?

correct.text: Reviewed local regulations and procedures and planned for them accordingly.
correct.why: Assessing mandated social impact procedures during planning would have kept the project compliant and prevented suspension.

distractors.text: Developed a contingency plan in case of suspension by the government.
distractors.trap: Plans for the symptom instead of preventing the breach.

distractors.text: Requested additional resources to speed construction after the suspension.
distractors.trap: Reacts after the fact and ignores the compliance root cause.

distractors.text: Established a regular communication channel with the local government.
distractors.trap: Communication alone does not ensure regulatory compliance.

explanation: Government-mandated regulations and procedures must be reviewed during planning, so assessing the social impact procedures up front would have kept the project compliant and avoided suspension.

prompts: A project manager forming a new hybrid team finds the best candidates are geographically dispersed, risking project performance. Colocation would sharply raise costs, and the company is already struggling financially amid severe competition. What should the project manager do?

correct.text: Ensure the right virtual collaboration tools are available to the team.
correct.why: Virtual collaboration tools enable needed teamwork economically without the cost of colocation.

distractors.text: Insist on colocating the team by cutting the budget elsewhere.
distractors.trap: Diverts scarce funds when virtual tools would suffice.

distractors.text: Ask the product owner for additional funds to support colocation.
distractors.trap: Requests money the struggling company cannot spare.

distractors.text: Organize the work strictly around the members' locations.
distractors.trap: Restructuring by location may not be feasible and avoids real collaboration.

explanation: Given the financial constraints, ensuring the team has the right virtual collaboration tools is the most economical way to enable the collaboration needed for a dispersed hybrid team.

prompts: A colocated team struggles with communication because some specialists arrive early and others leave late. At the last weekly meeting several were absent. How can the project manager best ensure everyone can attend the necessary meetings?

correct.text: Schedule team meetings during the overlapping core hours common to everyone.
correct.why: Meeting when all members are present ensures everyone receives the same information and can participate.

distractors.text: Encourage the specialists to all work the same hours.
distractors.trap: Forcing identical schedules may be infeasible and does not guarantee attendance.

distractors.text: Discuss what was missed separately with the absent specialists.
distractors.trap: Redundant and inefficient; it repeats information rather than solving attendance.

distractors.text: Create an information radiator to display meeting details publicly.
distractors.trap: Radiators share summaries but do not enable participation in the meeting.

explanation: Because members keep different hours, scheduling meetings during shared core hours lets everyone attend and receive the same message, directly resolving the attendance problem.

prompts: An agile team is rolling out a supporting technology platform across departments and is planning a readiness review before delivery. Who should the project manager propose to conduct the readiness review?

correct.text: The subject matter expert, who deeply understands the platform and departmental needs.
correct.why: Assessing whether the platform functions and meets department needs requires the SME's deep technical understanding.

distractors.text: The product owner, who owns the product vision and backlog prioritization.
distractors.trap: Focuses on vision and priorities, not deep technical readiness of the platform.

distractors.text: The quality control manager, who ensures delivery quality and efficiency.
distractors.trap: QC targets defects during development, not overall functional readiness across departments.

distractors.text: The quality assurance manager, who recommends delivery process improvements.
distractors.trap: QA confirms processes are followed; the review needs functional confirmation, not improvements.

explanation: A readiness review assesses whether the platform works as intended and meets departmental needs, which is best judged by the SME with deep knowledge of the platform and those needs.

prompts: During a sprint review, stakeholders appear uninformed and cannot agree on how to move the project forward. What should the project manager do first to ensure stakeholders can authorize the outputs?

correct.text: Review the stakeholder communications plan.
correct.why: Uninformed stakeholders signal a communication gap; reviewing the plan first identifies why their needs aren't being met.

distractors.text: Perform another systematic stakeholder analysis.
distractors.trap: May be needed, but not before checking whether the existing communication plan is sound.

distractors.text: Add the stakeholders to the daily sync meetings.
distractors.trap: Stakeholders may not attend and it doesn't fix the underlying communication gap.

distractors.text: Colocate the team to improve overall collaboration.
distractors.trap: Often infeasible and unrelated to the stakeholder information gap.

explanation: Because the communication plan is built around stakeholder needs, the project manager should first review it to find why those needs aren't being met, then adjust as required.

eco_task: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
topic: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
prompts: After the project manager provided video-based training for new software, several specialists reported feeling unprepared to use it. What should the project manager have done to ensure the training was effective?

correct.text: Assessed individual and team preferences for how they learn best.
correct.why: Understanding preferred learning styles lets the training be tailored so everyone feels prepared.

distractors.text: Tested the specialists' understanding after they finished the training.
distractors.trap: Testing measures knowledge but doesn't fix a training format that didn't work.

distractors.text: Asked the training department to source more suitable courses.
distractors.trap: Reactive; jumps to external options before understanding what the team needs.

distractors.text: Directed the specialists to learn the skills from the software manual.
distractors.trap: Manuals lack interactivity and engagement, leaving some learners behind.

explanation: Different people learn differently, so assessing individual and team learning preferences first lets the project manager adapt the training and ensure everyone is prepared.

eco_task: Promote Team Performance Through the Application of Emotional Intelligence
topic: Promote Team Performance Through the Application of Emotional Intelligence
prompts: A project is halfway complete with a good SPI, but the customer has flagged several minor issues that could be resolved quickly if they spoke directly with the development team. The project manager agrees, yet some specialists feel unsure about engaging the customer. How can the project manager support successful customer engagement?

correct.text: Assess members' communication styles and provide feedback to mentor the team.
correct.why: Mentoring communication skills builds the team's confidence to engage customers and resolve issues directly.

distractors.text: Ensure that all specialists clearly understand their roles and responsibilities.
distractors.trap: Role clarity isn't the issue; the gap is confidence in customer communication.

distractors.text: Assess the team's technical skills and provide additional development training.
distractors.trap: No evidence of a technical gap; the issue is engagement, not skills.

distractors.text: Revise the SPI and review all project team communications.
distractors.trap: Updating a metric doesn't address the team's apprehension about engaging customers.

explanation: The team's hesitation is about communicating with the customer, so assessing communication styles and mentoring the team is the most direct way to enable successful engagement.

prompts: You are managing a complex project with many stakeholders who hold conflicting priorities. What should you do to reduce conflict?

correct.text: Document all requirements and gain sign-off on the project deliverables.
correct.why: Clear documented agreement keeps stakeholders aligned and any changes flow through change control.

distractors.text: Take the lead personally on all aspects of the negotiations.
distractors.trap: Centralizing negotiation doesn't create the shared, documented alignment that prevents conflict.

distractors.text: Negotiate every aspect with help from the portfolio support office.
distractors.trap: Offloading negotiation doesn't replace documented, signed-off agreement among stakeholders.

distractors.text: Negotiate all aspects using agile to ensure easy deliveries.
distractors.trap: Method choice doesn't resolve conflicting priorities without documented agreement.

explanation: Documenting requirements and obtaining sign-off keeps stakeholders aligned to agreed priorities; any later changes are assessed through change control, preventing unnecessary conflict.

prompts: Project Manager B is taking over a project from Project Manager A, who says it is on schedule only because A constantly pushed the team hard. What is the first action Project Manager B should take?

correct.text: Determine a management strategy.
correct.why: The handover reveals a problematic leadership style, so B must first define how to lead going forward.

distractors.text: Check the risk status.
distractors.trap: A detail to review later, not the foundational first step after taking over.

distractors.text: Check the cost performance.
distractors.trap: Useful data, but it doesn't address how to lead the strained team.

distractors.text: Tell the team the new objectives.
distractors.trap: Premature; objectives follow from a defined management approach.

explanation: Given the coercive leadership that kept the project on schedule, the new project manager should first establish a management strategy before diving into specific metrics or directives.

prompts: On a new sales-report integration project, the lead sales manager and team miss the deadline for the status report, and the report also fails to meet the expectations set in the review meetings. Which action should come first?

correct.text: Assess the team's understanding of the action plan by asking for their feedback.
correct.why: Late and off-target work points to a comprehension gap; understanding it first addresses the root cause.

distractors.text: Confirm the plan is documented and shared with the team and stakeholders.
distractors.trap: The team already has the plan; the issue is understanding it, not access.

distractors.text: Increase the frequency of team meetings for the status report.
distractors.trap: More meetings may improve timeliness but not the report's quality or comprehension.

distractors.text: Give the specialists more time to deliberate and plan.
distractors.trap: Adding time doesn't resolve the underlying misunderstanding of the plan.

explanation: The late, sub-par report suggests the team doesn't understand the action plan, so the project manager should first gather feedback to find the root cause before taking further action.

prompts: A project manager leads with grace and confidence, offering support, listening attentively, and letting specialists work with little supervision. Which model of worker behavior does this project manager exhibit?

correct.text: Theory Y.
correct.why: Theory Y managers trust people to perform their best when given support and proper motivation.

distractors.text: Expectancy theory.
distractors.trap: Concerns how expected rewards drive effort, not this supportive supervisory style.

distractors.text: Theory X.
distractors.trap: Opposite view: assumes people avoid work and need constant supervision.

distractors.text: Theory Z.
distractors.trap: Focuses on building long-term employee loyalty, not this hands-off support style.

explanation: Theory Y managers believe people want to do their best given the right motivation; they support their teams, care about their people, and listen well, matching the described behavior.

prompts: You manage a complex project with many stakeholders and conflicting priorities. Senior management has told you to avoid political 'noise,' and resources are hard to secure. What should you do to keep the project progressing smoothly?

correct.text: Allocate the scarce resources efficiently to meet the project's needs.
correct.why: Resources are the top constraint for conflict; allocating them to project needs keeps progress steady.

distractors.text: Apply a 'do something nice for me and I'll do something nice for you' approach.
distractors.trap: Bases resourcing on personal favors rather than project needs.

distractors.text: Stay consistent in words and actions to avoid stakeholder conflict.
distractors.trap: Good conduct, but it doesn't resolve the actual resource scarcity.

distractors.text: Use compliments to obtain resources and cooperate toward mutual goals.
distractors.trap: Relies on flattery and personal tactics instead of prioritized allocation.

explanation: Resources are the leading source of project conflict, so allocating scarce resources efficiently to meet project needs, rather than serving personal tactics, is the priority for smooth progress.

prompts: You lead two highly motivated teams that juggle several short, tight-deadline projects at once. Communication, morale, and performance have recently declined across both teams, and you want to improve effectiveness. Which action should come first?

correct.text: Facilitate open communication sessions to identify and address the underlying challenges.
correct.why: Engaging both teams surfaces the root causes and empowers them to own the solutions.

distractors.text: Roll out a new collaboration tool to ease communication between the teams.
distractors.trap: A tool doesn't address the root cause of the communication breakdown.

distractors.text: Conduct individual performance reviews to address issues within each team.
distractors.trap: Focuses on individuals and ignores broader team and project-level problems.

distractors.text: Organize team-building activities to boost collaboration and morale.
distractors.trap: Addresses morale superficially without uncovering the underlying problems.

explanation: Open communication lets both teams voice concerns and help find root causes, fostering ownership; a tool, individual reviews, or team-building each address symptoms rather than the underlying issue.

eco_task: Engage Stakeholders and Build Shared Understanding
topic: Engage Stakeholders and Build Shared Understanding
prompts: An executive sponsor is increasingly anxious about the return on the project's investment and wants assurance the original dates will be met. What should the project manager do to convince the sponsor the project is on track?

correct.text: Present past forecasts alongside current performance.
correct.why: Comparing prior forecasts to actual performance shows whether the project is tracking to plan and value.

distractors.text: Emphasize the value delivered at the phase gate.
distractors.trap: Infrequent phase-gate value may not reassure the sponsor in a timely way.

distractors.text: Increase the number of project status reports.
distractors.trap: More reports add volume without directly addressing the sponsor's specific concern.

distractors.text: Invite the executive sponsor to daily project meetings.
distractors.trap: Often impractical and doesn't directly demonstrate on-track performance.

explanation: Comparing past forecasts with current performance demonstrates whether earlier projections reflect reality, directly showing the sponsor the likelihood of meeting dates and value.

prompts: An experienced team member tells the project manager about a conflict with another team member and asks for help. The project manager schedules a meeting with both specialists. What should the project manager do during the meeting to resolve the conflict?

correct.text: Work with them to determine a resolution together.
correct.why: Collaborating on a resolution repairs the relationship and moves the conflict into problem solving.

distractors.text: Mandate the solution unilaterally.
distractors.trap: Imposing a solution excludes the parties and rarely resolves conflict effectively.

distractors.text: Separate the two specialists.
distractors.trap: Separation is a last resort, not a first response to conflict.

distractors.text: Identify who instigated the issue.
distractors.trap: Assigning blame is inappropriate; the focus should be resolution, not fault.

explanation: Working with both specialists to reach a resolution together repairs the relationship and shifts the conflict into a collaborative problem-solving space, unlike mandating, separating, or assigning blame.

prompts: On your project, a previously low-influence stakeholder has just been promoted and is now a high-influence stakeholder. What should you update to ensure this stakeholder is properly engaged?

correct.text: Stakeholder engagement plan.
correct.why: It captures engagement strategies and must be updated when a stakeholder's influence changes.

distractors.text: Stakeholder communications plan.
distractors.trap: Governs message logistics, not the engagement strategy for a changed influence level.

distractors.text: Resource management plan.
distractors.trap: Deals with team and physical resources, unrelated to stakeholder engagement.

distractors.text: Risk management plan.
distractors.trap: Addresses risk approach, not how to engage a newly influential stakeholder.

explanation: The stakeholder engagement plan should be updated whenever a stakeholder's importance to project success changes, such as this promotion, to ensure proper involvement.

prompts: The executive sponsor has approved the project scope statement. The specialists want to modify the work breakdown structure without changing scope and send the request to the project manager. What should the project manager do with this request?

correct.text: Discuss it with the team and decide by consensus.
correct.why: Empowering the team to shape their work builds trust and produces better decisions and performance.

distractors.text: Decide which changes to make and inform the team.
distractors.trap: A unilateral decision that excludes and can demotivate the team.

distractors.text: Refuse the request and escalate it to the executive sponsor.
distractors.trap: Dismissive and non-collaborative; escalation is unnecessary since scope is unchanged.

distractors.text: Tell the team they cannot modify the established WBS.
distractors.trap: Unilateral refusal that ignores the team's views and expertise.

explanation: Since scope is unchanged, the project manager should empower the team by discussing the WBS change and reaching consensus, rather than making unilateral decisions or escalating unnecessarily.

eco_task: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
topic: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
prompts: A project is behind schedule when a high-performing new member is assigned. Assessing them, the project manager finds they lack the specific project training but have the skills and relevant experience to contribute. Which response is best?

correct.text: Assign the new member to noncritical activities without close monitoring while training is provided.
correct.why: It contributes to the project immediately without risking critical work, while training proceeds.

distractors.text: Ensure the member completes training before joining the team.
distractors.trap: Delays their contribution, which is inefficient on a project already behind.

distractors.text: Assign the member to critical activities under close monitoring while training.
distractors.trap: Places an untrained member on critical work, risking the schedule further.

distractors.text: Decline adding the new member to the team.
distractors.trap: Wasteful given their skills and relevant experience could help recover schedule.

explanation: Assigning the new member to noncritical work while training is provided lets them contribute right away without endangering critical activities; critical assignments follow once competence is demonstrated.

eco_task: Ensure Knowledge Transfer for Project Continuity
topic: Ensure Knowledge Transfer for Project Continuity
prompts: The team has flagged that the project lacks clear knowledge-transfer expectations. Which of the following is NOT an obstacle to effective knowledge transfer?

correct.text: A systematic review of the completed work
correct.why: A structured review is itself a knowledge-transfer activity, not a barrier to it.

distractors.text: Reluctance to share personal failures and mistakes
distractors.trap: Fear of exposing errors genuinely blocks honest lessons-learned sharing.

distractors.text: Insufficient individual motivation to document lessons
distractors.trap: Low motivation to record lessons is a real obstacle to transfer.

distractors.text: Sparse leadership involvement in promoting transfer
distractors.trap: Weak leadership support genuinely undermines a knowledge-sharing culture.

explanation: Reluctance, low motivation, and weak leadership all obstruct knowledge transfer; a systematic review is a transfer mechanism, so it is the exception.

eco_task: Engage Stakeholders and Build Shared Understanding
topic: Engage Stakeholders and Build Shared Understanding
prompts: A client's interactions with the project team have turned negative, and several delivery specialists no longer want to be part of the project. Which response fits best?

correct.text: Negotiate with the client and the team to settle the differences.
correct.why: Balancing both parties' concerns through negotiation resolves the friction while keeping the project on course.

distractors.text: Meet only with the client to discuss the team's concerns.
distractors.trap: One-sided engagement ignores the team's need to be heard directly.

distractors.text: Defer entirely to the client's expectations and sentiments.
distractors.trap: Prioritizing the client alone abandons the dissatisfied team.

distractors.text: Assign all delivery specialists to stakeholder engagement tasks.
distractors.trap: Reassigning work does not address the underlying interpersonal conflict.

explanation: The PM must balance the client's priorities against the team's dissatisfaction, and negotiating with both to settle differences is the collaborative resolution.

prompts: On a fixed-budget construction project, costs are rising because the team is using more materials than the scope allows. The cause is that two specialists new to the organization have not been reporting the materials they acquire. What should the project manager have done to prevent this?

correct.text: Provided training on the material acquisition and reporting process.
correct.why: The new members lacked awareness of the process; training would have ensured correct acquisition and reporting.

distractors.text: Documented a materials cost-deviation risk in the risk register.
distractors.trap: Logging a risk tracks the problem but does not prevent the reporting gap.

distractors.text: Set aside a contingency reserve to absorb materials overruns.
distractors.trap: Reserves fund overruns; they do not stop the behavior causing them.

distractors.text: Excluded new engineers from a fixed-budget strategic project.
distractors.trap: Barring new staff is impractical and forfeits their skills.

explanation: The overruns stem from new specialists not understanding the acquisition and reporting process, so onboarding training is the true preventive measure.

prompts: Mid-way through an incremental delivery project, the team asks a subject matter expert from another department to join and help with a specific task, then informs the project leader. Which response fits best?

correct.text: Coordinate the needed resources based on the team's working agreement.
correct.why: Cross-team resource coordination through the working agreement is the established, empowering way to bring in help.

distractors.text: Check the budget and assess the feasibility of adding the resource.
distractors.trap: A budget check ignores team fabric and the SME's ramp-up impact.

distractors.text: Get the executive sponsor's approval, stressing the resource is crucial.
distractors.trap: Escalating to the sponsor bypasses normal cross-team coordination.

distractors.text: Ask the SME's functional manager about their availability first.
distractors.trap: Going straight to the functional manager skips the team's agreed process.

explanation: Teams routinely coordinate with others across the organization; the project leader should facilitate that coordination using the team's working agreement rather than escalating.

eco_task: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
topic: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
prompts: You are eight months into a complex project. Your team is skilled, highly motivated, and consistently successful. What management and leadership style best fits the team at this stage?

correct.text: Empower the team to improve results
correct.why: A mature, motivated team performs best with autonomy and empowerment rather than close direction.

distractors.text: Support with periodic iterations and feedback
distractors.trap: Useful, but less impactful than granting a proven team autonomy.

distractors.text: Focus on resolving conflicts within the team
distractors.trap: Conflict management is secondary for a team already working well.

distractors.text: Offer feedback and coaching only when asked
distractors.trap: Purely reactive support underuses the PM's leadership role.

explanation: A capable, motivated, successful team thrives under empowerment; delegating authority to improve results best sustains their high performance.

eco_task: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
topic: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
prompts: Specialists moved from a predictive project to a newly formed agile team are complaining in the team chat about the extra training they must complete. How should the project manager handle this?

correct.text: Meet to hear the concerns and explain why the training matters.
correct.why: Open dialogue lets the PM address grievances and clarify the training's value collaboratively.

distractors.text: Ignore the complaints and assume the team adapts over time.
distractors.trap: Passivity lets dissatisfaction fester and morale drop.

distractors.text: Drop the training requirement to relieve the dissatisfaction.
distractors.trap: Removing needed training leaves the team unprepared for agile.

distractors.text: Send a memo mandating the training and warning of consequences.
distractors.trap: An authoritarian memo escalates resentment instead of resolving it.

explanation: Meeting the team to listen and explain the training's importance addresses the concern directly and fosters a positive environment, unlike ignoring, removing, or mandating.

prompts: Your organization favors virtual teams, where new challenges arise regularly, and you are in the early stages of a new agile project. What should the project manager do to help the team overcome these challenges?

correct.text: Run a SWOT analysis to pinpoint the team's status and act.
correct.why: A SWOT analysis quickly diagnoses the team's gaps so the PM can take targeted action.

distractors.text: Halt all activity and gather the team to discuss the challenges.
distractors.trap: Stopping the project overreacts before the issues are understood.

distractors.text: Wait for the next daily scrum to raise the challenges with everyone.
distractors.trap: Delaying to the daily scrum defers needed diagnosis.

distractors.text: Partner with the product owner to decide how to remove impediments.
distractors.trap: These challenges are internal to the team, not the product owner's role.

explanation: The PM must first understand the team's problems and gaps; a SWOT analysis provides that diagnosis before stopping work or involving the product owner.

prompts: A new member joins during execution, and the work demands strong collaboration with the other specialists. What should the project manager do to foster a collaborative environment?

correct.text: Hold a one-day team workshop to bring the new member up to speed.
correct.why: A facilitated workshop actively builds relationships, alignment, and knowledge transfer across the whole team.

distractors.text: Email the team asking them to collaborate with the new member.
distractors.trap: A passive request does not build real connections.

distractors.text: Share all information and explain the work in progress in detail.
distractors.trap: Information dumping overlooks relationship and team dynamics.

distractors.text: Ask each member to share their interests at the next meeting.
distractors.trap: Introductions alone do not address integration needs.

explanation: A one-day workshop actively facilitates knowledge transfer, relationship building, and alignment, laying a stronger foundation for collaboration than the passive alternatives.

eco_task: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
topic: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
prompts: Agile specialists just finished in-person training on several critical, complex processes. The next day, several say they are struggling to remember what they learned. Another session is available next week, but the team is behind on critical tasks and the training takes a full day. What should the project manager do?

correct.text: Create an information radiator with the training's key takeaways.
correct.why: A visible radiator keeps the key information at a glance, reinforcing recall without lost workdays.

distractors.text: Have specialists pair up to share what they each learned.
distractors.trap: Peer sharing risks incomplete or misremembered information.

distractors.text: Invite specialists to reattend the training for a refresher.
distractors.trap: A second full workday deepens the schedule slippage.

distractors.text: Point specialists to the training's review notes when needed.
distractors.trap: Notes are less visible and accessible than a radiator.

explanation: An information radiator posts key takeaways where everyone can see them at a glance, reinforcing recall in an agile setting without spending another full training day.

prompts: A global, cross-functional team has been assembled to deliver a multinational initiative. What should the project manager do to ensure full team participation throughout the project?

correct.text: Coordinate time-zone information and agree on meeting times.
correct.why: Meeting times that fit a global calendar let everyone actually attend and participate.

distractors.text: Ensure the software is delivered in multiple languages.
distractors.trap: Localized software does not affect who can attend meetings.

distractors.text: Translate the project documentation for the team.
distractors.trap: Translation assumes a language gap the internal team may not have.

distractors.text: Provide guidance on common tools and practices.
distractors.trap: Colleagues in one company already share tools and practices.

explanation: Participation across a global team hinges on accommodating time zones; agreeing on meeting times that fit a global calendar is what actually enables everyone to take part.

prompts: A new project manager joins an organization that has never had formal project management. Some specialists refuse to do basic tasks like owning action items from meetings. Which response fits best?

correct.text: Hold a team meeting on collaborative problem-solving to agree on how tasks get done.
correct.why: Involving the whole team in shaping the approach builds the buy-in needed to change behavior.

distractors.text: Meet privately with the resistant specialists about PM's benefits.
distractors.trap: One-on-ones miss the team-wide buy-in a shared approach needs.

distractors.text: Write an internal document explaining project management's benefits.
distractors.trap: A document does not create ownership or team culture.

distractors.text: Discuss project management's benefits with the organization's executives.
distractors.trap: A top-down mandate is not the PM's first path to team buy-in.

explanation: Collaborative problem-solving invites input from all specialists and builds the ownership needed to change behavior, unlike private talks, documents, or executive mandates.

prompts: As project manager of a culturally diverse team, you discover major intra-team conflicts during execution. Which response fits best?

correct.text: Schedule a team meeting to understand the conflicts.
correct.why: Meeting to understand the conflicts gets to their root and involves the team in solutions.

distractors.text: Advise the specialists to work together harmoniously.
distractors.trap: Telling people to get along ignores the root cause.

distractors.text: Revise the resource management plan.
distractors.trap: A plan update does not resolve interpersonal conflict.

distractors.text: Put the members into team-building activities.
distractors.trap: Activities skip diagnosing what the conflicts actually are.

explanation: The PM must first understand the conflicts and involve the team in identifying and resolving them, rather than issuing advice, editing a plan, or jumping to team-building.

prompts: In an agile environment, a project leader should always set aside time for open, free discussion among specialists about how the team can raise its performance. Why is this practice important for the team?

correct.text: Teams should continuously seek to improve their processes.
correct.why: Empowering the team to improve its own processes drives sustainable performance gains.

distractors.text: Teams should continuously act to increase their output pace.
distractors.trap: Chasing pace alone is an outcome, not the reason for reflection.

distractors.text: Teams should continuously experiment with new ways of working.
distractors.trap: Experimentation without analysis can add unmanaged risk.

distractors.text: Teams should continuously push members to work harder.
distractors.trap: Pushing harder erodes morale and motivation.

explanation: Open reflection lets the team analyze and continuously improve its own processes with accountability and pride, which is more sustainable than chasing pace, experimenting blindly, or pushing harder.

prompts: The team is actively preparing the final delivery before the product goes live in production. What should the project manager do to ensure the whole team stays in full sync?

correct.text: Ensure the team attends daily meetings and participates actively.
correct.why: Daily meetings keep everyone aligned so there are no surprises before go-live.

distractors.text: Ensure the needed information is available and accessible to everyone.
distractors.trap: Available information does not by itself create active alignment.

distractors.text: Ensure the work is visualized on a board accessible to all.
distractors.trap: A visible board supports sync but does not replace daily coordination.

distractors.text: Ensure a space exists for the team to talk whenever needed.
distractors.trap: Ad hoc conversation lacks the cadence that keeps the team synced.

explanation: Active participation in daily meetings keeps the team on track and eliminates waiting and hand-off delays, making it the best way to stay in sync before go-live.

prompts: You manage a project at a fast-growing startup that offers flexible hours and work-from-home. In the last retrospective, a specialist observed that velocity improved when the team was physically together in the project room. Which response fits best?

correct.text: Gather feedback and analyze location's impact on team velocity.
correct.why: Validating the observation with data lets the PM make a well-informed decision about colocation.

distractors.text: Refine the backlog to boost velocity and keep current policies.
distractors.trap: Backlog work sidesteps the raised question about location.

distractors.text: Share the idea with senior management and suggest new policies.
distractors.trap: Proposing policy change is premature before validating the claim.

distractors.text: Revise the resource plan and charter to require colocation.
distractors.trap: Mandating colocation acts on one unverified observation.

explanation: One retrospective comment is not proof; the PM should gather feedback and analyze the impact of location on velocity before making decisions or proposing policy changes.

prompts: In a side conversation, the product owner tells the project leader they are concerned about how the architecture owner is handling the upcoming delivery. What should the project leader do to address this?

correct.text: Ensure the specialists use daily meetings to discuss and resolve issues.
correct.why: Surfacing the concern in daily meetings lets the whole team weigh in and resolve it collaboratively.

distractors.text: Reassure the product owner not to worry and invite them to daily meetings.
distractors.trap: Reassurance dismisses the concern instead of addressing it.

distractors.text: Share the concern with the architecture owner and ask their view.
distractors.trap: A one-on-one omits the other specialists' perspectives.

distractors.text: Ask the product owner to step back until after the delivery.
distractors.trap: Sidelining the product owner ignores a valid, in-scope concern.

explanation: Agile relies on team discussion to resolve issues; raising the concern in daily meetings brings all perspectives together, unlike reassuring, going one-on-one, or deferring it.

eco_task: Promote Team Performance Through the Application of Emotional Intelligence
topic: Promote Team Performance Through the Application of Emotional Intelligence
prompts: A customer makes weekly visits to inspect the final stage of product development. The quality manager reports that on previous visits the customer was very unprofessional and reprimanded the specialists. What should the project manager do to handle this?

correct.text: Understand the situation by speaking with the specialists and the customer.
correct.why: Gathering facts from both sides lets the PM define the problem before choosing a response.

distractors.text: Draft a letter complaining about the customer inspector's behavior.
distractors.trap: Complaining reacts before the facts are gathered.

distractors.text: Let the week pass, then send a complaint to the customer.
distractors.trap: Waiting and complaining still skips understanding the issue.

distractors.text: Take the matter to the PM's senior management team.
distractors.trap: Escalating precedes understanding what actually happened.

explanation: The PM should resolve the conflict by defining the problem, gathering facts from both the team and the customer, and analyzing before acting, rather than reacting to a partly understood situation.

prompts: The communication management plan specifies phone calls only for a key stakeholder. Three weeks into execution, that stakeholder begins sending an increasing volume of redundant, ad-hoc requests. How should you handle this?

correct.text: Meet with the stakeholder to discuss their communication needs and expectations.
correct.why: A conversation uncovers why the requests are occurring and lets you realign communications to the root need.

distractors.text: Continue phone-call communication since it has been effective so far.
distractors.trap: Ignores the signal that current communication is not meeting the need.

distractors.text: Email the stakeholder requesting a change in communication approach.
distractors.trap: Lacks the depth to uncover the underlying expectations gap.

distractors.text: Assign a team member as liaison between you and the stakeholder.
distractors.trap: Delegates a relationship the PM should own.

explanation: A surge of redundant ad-hoc requests signals that communication planning is not meeting the stakeholder's needs; meeting with them uncovers the cause and realigns the approach. Continuing as-is, emailing, or delegating do not address the root need.

eco_task: Engage Stakeholders and Build Shared Understanding
topic: Engage Stakeholders and Build Shared Understanding
prompts: You are planning a complex, high-environmental-impact project authorized by the PMO. The sponsor has given you a complete list of project stakeholders. What is the next stakeholder-management activity?

correct.text: Analyze
correct.why: With stakeholders already identified, the next step is to analyze their influence and impact before engaging or monitoring.

distractors.text: Monitor
distractors.trap: Comes later, after stakeholders are analyzed and engaged.

distractors.text: Engage
distractors.trap: Cannot engage effectively before analyzing influence and interest.

distractors.text: Identify
distractors.trap: Already done; the sponsor provided the full stakeholder list.

explanation: Since the sponsor already supplied the full stakeholder list, identification is complete and the PM must next analyze stakeholders' influence and impact before engaging and monitoring them.

prompts: The final project schedule is delayed because you cannot reach an agreement with a vendor essential to delivery. Which response is best?

correct.text: Meet with the vendor to find a path forward and identify an alternative if needed.
correct.why: It seeks agreement first while keeping a backup vendor option, protecting delivery.

distractors.text: Circulate meeting minutes and review agreed and open clauses with the team and customer.
distractors.trap: Documents the situation but does not resolve the stalled negotiation.

distractors.text: Meet the sponsor and seek support to replace the vendor over the failed talks.
distractors.trap: Premature escalation; replacement is typically a last resort.

distractors.text: Pressure the vendor to accept the terms quickly for successful delivery.
distractors.trap: Coercion does not guarantee a timely or satisfactory agreement.

explanation: The PM should try to come to terms with the vendor and, if that fails, seek alternatives, keeping delivery on track. Circulating minutes, escalating to the sponsor, or pressuring the vendor do not resolve the impasse constructively.

prompts: You lead a virtual team across several countries and time zones. Members are missing deadlines and failing to produce expected outputs due to irregular communication. How should you resolve this?

correct.text: Engage with the team members to understand their challenges.
correct.why: Understanding the specific obstacles must precede any targeted solution for a virtual team.

distractors.text: Provide the team with a deliverables list to guide them.
distractors.trap: Assumes the cause is unclear tasks before diagnosing the real issue.

distractors.text: Arrange time-management training for the team.
distractors.trap: Presumes a skills gap rather than investigating the communication problem.

distractors.text: Take steps to colocate the team in the same office.
distractors.trap: Impractical for a distributed team and skips understanding the cause.

explanation: Engaging virtual team members to understand their challenges is essential before acting, because the PM can only resolve issues once aware of them. Providing lists, training, or colocation are premature until the causes are understood.

eco_task: Ensure Knowledge Transfer for Project Continuity
topic: Ensure Knowledge Transfer for Project Continuity
prompts: A hybrid project uses an iterative design process, but separate teams for design and execution are causing concerns. What should you do to address these concerns?

correct.text: Establish a centralized repository for project documentation and product validation.
correct.why: A shared repository enables knowledge transfer and collaboration across the two teams.

distractors.text: Make the design team responsible for execution as well.
distractors.trap: May not be feasible and does not improve cross-team collaboration.

distractors.text: Tell the PMO that a hybrid approach is too risky.
distractors.trap: Unfounded and unhelpful; hybrid is not the source of the concern.

distractors.text: Provide training on an adaptive project management framework.
distractors.trap: Assumes a skills gap with no evidence the teams lack skill.

explanation: A centralized repository for documentation and validation bridges the separate design and execution teams by enabling knowledge transfer and collaboration. Merging responsibilities, blaming the hybrid approach, or adding training do not address the coordination gap.

eco_task: Engage Stakeholders and Build Shared Understanding
topic: Engage Stakeholders and Build Shared Understanding
prompts: You have just completed initiation and must identify and analyze stakeholders. The project charter addressed only scope and budget approval. What should you do to identify stakeholders?

correct.text: Review lessons learned from previous projects.
correct.why: Historical lessons reveal stakeholders involved in similar projects who may have an interest here.

distractors.text: Examine the project charter.
distractors.trap: This charter covers only scope and budget, not stakeholders.

distractors.text: Conduct a brainstorming session with the project team.
distractors.trap: The team may lack full understanding of scope and objectives this early.

distractors.text: Examine the organization chart.
distractors.trap: Not all stakeholders appear on the org chart.

explanation: Because the charter omits stakeholders and neither the team nor the org chart is comprehensive, reviewing lessons learned from similar past projects best surfaces stakeholders with a likely interest in this one.

prompts: You observe that team members are not collaborating or being proactive. What should you have done at the start of the project to create a better team environment?

correct.text: Conducted an assessment of team dynamics.
correct.why: Assessing dynamics early reveals strengths, weaknesses, and issues so they can be addressed upfront.

distractors.text: Assigned all tasks at the beginning of the project.
distractors.trap: Front-loading assignments does not build collaboration or proactivity.

distractors.text: Facilitated additional team meetings.
distractors.trap: More meetings do not address the underlying dynamics.

distractors.text: Split the project into more iterations.
distractors.trap: Restructuring work does not resolve interpersonal collaboration issues.

explanation: Assessing team dynamics at the outset would have revealed the team's strengths, weaknesses, and potential friction, letting the PM foster collaboration early. Assigning tasks, adding meetings, or adding iterations do not address the root cause.

prompts: As a PM you make decisions daily, whatever their importance or criticality. What should you do to keep those decisions unbiased and rational?

correct.text: Recognize that they cannot achieve everything.
correct.why: Acknowledging limits counters optimism bias, a common distortion in decision-making.

distractors.text: Use data without verifying its accuracy.
distractors.trap: Unverified data injects error into decisions rather than removing bias.

distractors.text: Make decisions quickly.
distractors.trap: Speed encourages hasty, biased judgments.

distractors.text: Make decisions only when outcomes are certain.
distractors.trap: Certainty is rarely available; waiting for it stalls decisions.

explanation: Recognizing they cannot achieve everything guards against optimism bias, the tendency to overestimate favorable outcomes. Using unverified data, rushing, or waiting for certainty all undermine rational decisions.

prompts: An IT contract requires all team members to be certified in IT security. You discover that one member presented a fake certificate. What should you do first to handle this?

correct.text: Log the issue in the issue tracker and remove the member from the project.
correct.why: Fraudulent documentation is unethical and breaches contract, so documenting and removing the member comes first.

distractors.text: Update the project management plan to add resources to certify the member.
distractors.trap: Rewards falsified credentials instead of addressing the ethical breach.

distractors.text: Verify that the remaining team members hold valid certificates.
distractors.trap: A later step; does not address the immediate violation.

distractors.text: Investigate why this was not discovered earlier.
distractors.trap: Important later, but not the immediate first action.

explanation: The fake certificate is an ethical and contractual breach, so the PM must first log the issue and remove the member. Verifying other certificates and investigating the gap are appropriate follow-up steps, not the first action.

prompts: At the end of the first phase of a stadium expansion, you realize the next phase will run behind schedule because of new safety requirements. Stakeholders are frustrated they were not warned of the possible safety changes. What should you have done to avoid this and keep stakeholders aware of potential disruptions?

correct.text: Perform a proactive risk assessment during initiation to identify potential safety delays.
correct.why: Early risk assessment surfaces safety-related delays so they can be communicated to stakeholders in advance.

distractors.text: Increase the project's contingency to absorb unforeseen safety issues.
distractors.trap: Cushions impact but neither prevents delays nor informs stakeholders.

distractors.text: Hold regular meetings with the site supervisor on safety needs.
distractors.trap: Helpful but insufficient to identify all potential safety delays.

distractors.text: Request additional resources to handle safety concerns as they arise.
distractors.trap: Reactive; does not foresee or communicate delays beforehand.

explanation: A proactive risk assessment during initiation identifies potential safety-related delays early, letting the PM communicate them to stakeholders before they materialize. Contingency, supervisor meetings, and extra resources do not proactively surface and communicate the risks.

prompts: In a five-year program, many members have joined and left the subprojects, and conflict among them occurs frequently. Which technique should the PM use to resolve the conflicts over the long term?

correct.text: Problem-solving
correct.why: Collaborating to find a solution addressing all viewpoints builds lasting trust and commitment (win-win).

distractors.text: Forcing
distractors.trap: Wins for one party at others' expense, breeding future conflict (win-lose).

distractors.text: Smoothing
distractors.trap: Avoids tough issues by stressing commonalities; conflict resurfaces.

distractors.text: Compromise
distractors.trap: Each side gives up something; no one is fully satisfied (lose-lose).

explanation: Problem-solving (collaborating) works with all parties to reach a solution incorporating multiple viewpoints, reinforcing trust and commitment for a durable win-win. Forcing, smoothing, and compromising do not resolve conflict lastingly.

prompts: A project needs a specialized vendor to install a complex, expensive piece of equipment. A week ago you emailed a partner requesting that a specialist come on-site, but the vendor has not replied to confirm. Which communication method was used here?

correct.text: Push
correct.why: Email is push communication: you can confirm it was sent, but not that it was received or understood.

distractors.text: Pull
distractors.trap: Pull requires the recipient to retrieve information from a repository at will.

distractors.text: Interactive
distractors.trap: Interactive needs real-time, two-way exchange, which email lacks.

distractors.text: Interpersonal
distractors.trap: Not a category of communication methods in this model.

explanation: Email is a push method: the sender can confirm it was sent but not that it was received or understood, which is why a follow-up was needed. Pull, interactive, and interpersonal do not describe a one-way email.

eco_task: Ensure Knowledge Transfer for Project Continuity
topic: Ensure Knowledge Transfer for Project Continuity
prompts: In an agile environment, you realize some new team members struggle to understand how more senior members achieved certain results on the project. Which response is best?

correct.text: Encourage and facilitate peer mentoring.
correct.why: Peer mentoring transfers tacit know-how from senior to new members and builds collaboration.

distractors.text: Arrange a review meeting with stakeholders.
distractors.trap: Stakeholders are not the source of the internal knowledge gap.

distractors.text: Ensure team members focus on their own tasks.
distractors.trap: Ignores the knowledge gap rather than closing it.

distractors.text: Assess the need for knowledge transfer.
distractors.trap: Analysis is slower; the need is already evident and mentoring acts on it.

explanation: Peer mentoring lets new members learn directly from experienced ones how results were achieved, while fostering collaboration. A stakeholder meeting or task focus ignores the gap, and assessing the need is less practical than acting on the evident need.

prompts: You lead a hybrid project. Several members recently left, including the only person with a specific critical skill. At the start of a new sprint you identify a task that needs that missing skill. What is the most appropriate next step?

correct.text: Assess the impact of the missing skill on the project.
correct.why: Understanding the impact first lets the PM choose the right response among several options.

distractors.text: Rescope the project to remove the task needing the missing skill.
distractors.trap: Drops scope prematurely before assessing whether it is necessary.

distractors.text: Delay the task until a replacement is recruited.
distractors.trap: Assumes recruitment is the only option before evaluating impact.

distractors.text: Reassign the task to another project team with the skill.
distractors.trap: May not be feasible and skips assessing the actual impact.

explanation: The PM should first assess the impact of the missing skill, then decide among options such as sourcing the skill internally, bringing in an external resource, or succession planning. Rescoping, delaying, or reassigning skip that assessment and may not be feasible.

prompts: A project hits a series of unforeseen issues affecting schedule and budget, several needing immediate resolution before interdependent issues can be addressed. The communication management plan states the client and key internal stakeholders receive only monthly reports. How should you communicate the impact of these issues?

correct.text: Increase communication with the client and stakeholders as needed to manage the issues and their impact.
correct.why: Urgent cost and schedule impacts must be shared immediately so affected parties can act, regardless of the reporting cadence.

distractors.text: Log the issues in the project issue tracker so they are captured internally for the record.
distractors.trap: Recording internally documents but does not inform stakeholders or resolve anything.

distractors.text: Keep to the monthly client and stakeholder reports specified in the communications plan.
distractors.trap: Rigidly following the plan delays notice of issues that need immediate action.

distractors.text: Revise the communications plan and wait for approval before notifying stakeholders.
distractors.trap: Seeking plan approval first wastes time these urgent issues do not allow.

explanation: Issues with immediate cost and schedule impact must be communicated as soon as flagged, even if the plan calls for monthly reporting; the PM adapts communication so the right parties can resolve them quickly.

prompts: Despite several requirements-gathering efforts and engagement during status meetings, the team tells you they are still unsure of the stakeholders' needs. How should you, as project manager, address this?

correct.text: Document what the team believes the stakeholders need, show it to stakeholders for feedback, and share the results with the team.
correct.why: Confirming the team's understanding directly with stakeholders aligns both sides and closes the gap collaboratively.

distractors.text: Require the team to work with stakeholders to analyze detailed needs and sign off on a specification.
distractors.trap: The team already engaged repeatedly; more of the same ignores that it did not work.

distractors.text: Discuss cancelling the project with the team since the uncertainty is too risky to continue.
distractors.trap: Early uncertainty is normal; cancelling avoids rather than resolves the disconnect.

distractors.text: Have the team write backlog items to capture the needs, then prioritize and manage the stories.
distractors.trap: Capturing needs without stakeholder validation still leaves the understanding unconfirmed.

explanation: When the team remains unsure of stakeholder needs after repeated efforts, documenting their understanding and validating it directly with stakeholders realigns everyone and prevents building the wrong thing.

eco_task: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
topic: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
prompts: As project manager, you realize the team needs training on a specific technology that the project requires. What should you do to reduce this risk?

correct.text: Assess the team's knowledge of the technology and identify recommended training options.
correct.why: Proactively evaluating skills and arranging training ensures the team can deliver with the required technology.

distractors.text: Contract an expert provider to do the portion of work that uses that technology.
distractors.trap: Outsourcing the work leaves the team unable to use the technology themselves.

distractors.text: Create an item in the risk log and inform the sponsor.
distractors.trap: Logging the risk identifies it but does not actively address the skills gap.

distractors.text: Fix any training gaps as they arise during the project.
distractors.trap: A reactive approach risks disruption instead of preventing it.

explanation: The proactive response is to assess the team's current knowledge and arrange suitable training so the required skills are in place before they are needed.

prompts: During a retrospective, team members appear disengaged, saying they keep repeating the same ideas over and over. How should you best handle this situation?

correct.text: Encourage the team to propose process improvements and support changes based on the feedback received.
correct.why: Acting on their improvement ideas shows the retrospective drives real change, restoring engagement.

distractors.text: Ask the team to pay more attention to the session even if they think they know what is coming.
distractors.trap: Implies inattention and ignores that repetition is the real cause.

distractors.text: Ask members to give their feedback even if it duplicates others', to emphasize the points.
distractors.trap: Encouraging repetition reinforces the very cause of the disengagement.

distractors.text: Encourage members to provide better feedback since they already know the questions.
distractors.trap: Suggests past feedback was inadequate, discouraging future participation.

explanation: Disengagement from repetition is resolved by encouraging the team to identify improvements and then acting on them, so retrospectives feel worthwhile rather than repetitive.

prompts: As project manager, you notice some team members arriving late, missing deadlines, skipping meetings, and producing low-quality work. What action should you take?

correct.text: Investigate the reasons for the disengaged behavior and work to boost morale and participation.
correct.why: Understanding the root cause lets the PM address the disengagement and restore performance.

distractors.text: Ask the team members to change how they work to match the newest work trends.
distractors.trap: Changing methods ignores the underlying cause of disengagement.

distractors.text: Encourage the rest of the team to double their efforts to cover the slack.
distractors.trap: Shifting the burden is unfair and leaves the real problem unresolved.

distractors.text: Ask the team members to abandon the process that is slowing their progress.
distractors.trap: Dropping process without diagnosis can create new problems later.

explanation: The PM must first understand why the members are disengaged, then take targeted steps to boost morale and participation, since motivated members are more productive.

prompts: After an annual staff review, a company decides to train all staff to face new challenges. Training will be delivered through a website where all content, exams, templates, and other organizational assets are available for staff to access. Which communication method is being used?

correct.text: Pull
correct.why: Recipients access posted content at their own convenience, which defines pull communication.

distractors.text: Push
distractors.trap: Push sends information directly to recipients rather than letting them retrieve it.

distractors.text: Interactive
distractors.trap: Interactive implies real-time two-way exchange, not self-service access.

distractors.text: Interpersonal
distractors.trap: Interpersonal is direct person-to-person contact, not a repository.

explanation: Posting content to a site that staff log into at their convenience is pull communication, where recipients passively access information on their own rather than receiving it in real time.

prompts: You work in a matrix organization. After a reorganization, several key project resources are reassigned to another project, leaving your project without the resources needed to deliver successfully. Which response fits best?

correct.text: Review the project's resource needs with the functional manager.
correct.why: In a matrix, resources report to functional managers, so the PM works with them to minimize the impact.

distractors.text: Refuse to release the newly reassigned resources.
distractors.trap: Refusing exceeds the PM's authority in a matrix organization.

distractors.text: Examine the stakeholder communications approach.
distractors.trap: Communications planning does not resolve the resource shortage.

distractors.text: Review resource allocation and utilization plans.
distractors.trap: Reviewing plans does not restore the resources that were removed.

explanation: In a matrix, shared resources report to functional managers, so the PM reviews resource needs with the functional manager, explaining the impact and working to minimize it.

eco_task: Engage Stakeholders and Build Shared Understanding
topic: Engage Stakeholders and Build Shared Understanding
prompts: During a kick-off meeting, the sponsor says they cannot attend any meetings for the first half of the project and wants to assign a replacement. Which response fits best?

correct.text: Analyze the impact of the change and work with the sponsor to identify a suitable replacement.
correct.why: Assessing consequences and collaborating on the replacement is the most proactive, comprehensive approach.

distractors.text: Request that the replacement holds substantial influence and decision-making authority.
distractors.trap: Naming one trait ignores other needed qualities and relies solely on the sponsor's pick.

distractors.text: Set up a meeting with the replacement as soon as possible to discuss the agenda.
distractors.trap: Jumping to agenda talk skips analyzing the impact of the absence.

distractors.text: Request the sponsor's full attention and escalate the issue to the portfolio support office.
distractors.trap: Demanding attendance and escalating is premature and likely infeasible.

explanation: The PM should analyze the impact of the sponsor's absence, such as delays and engagement gaps, and collaborate with the sponsor to select a suitable replacement who can provide the needed support.

prompts: A delivery milestone is fast approaching and a planned item is at risk. A team member becomes defensive and starts blaming others for the problem. What should you, as project manager, have done earlier to prevent this?

correct.text: Established ground rules for interaction that the whole team must follow.
correct.why: Clear ground rules set respectful interaction norms that discourage finger-pointing when pressure rises.

distractors.text: Established and maintained meaningful relationships between team members.
distractors.trap: Good relationships help but do not set explicit norms against blaming.

distractors.text: Established formal patterns of authority, responsibility, and accountability.
distractors.trap: Formal structures clarify roles but do not govern respectful behavior.

distractors.text: Established clear delegation and accountability-sharing principles.
distractors.trap: Delegation clarity does not prevent defensive, blame-shifting behavior.

explanation: Ground rules define how the team interacts respectfully, creating a safe environment that prevents the finger-pointing and blame that surfaces under milestone pressure.

prompts: You lead a cross-functional team. During a brainstorming session, you notice some members do not see the need for a change, and this is hurting the team's output. Which response fits best?

correct.text: Engage the members to understand their doubts and gain their buy-in.
correct.why: Addressing their concerns directly wins them over and improves the team's output.

distractors.text: Restate the objectives of the project to the team.
distractors.trap: Repeating objectives may not convince members who already resist the change.

distractors.text: Ask the executive sponsor to review the project charter.
distractors.trap: The charter will not address why specific members resist the change.

distractors.text: End the brainstorming session and switch to interview methods.
distractors.trap: Abandoning the session skips the direct engagement that should come first.

explanation: When members resist a change, the PM should engage them to understand their doubts and earn buy-in, improving output and the project's chances before trying other methods.

eco_task: Ensure Knowledge Transfer for Project Continuity
topic: Ensure Knowledge Transfer for Project Continuity
prompts: After consulting executive stakeholders, you decide to replace a vendor during the execution phase. The new vendor has more domain knowledge and, while not identified earlier, is likely the right candidate now. Which document would contain the best information for the new vendor?

correct.text: Request for Proposal (RFP)
correct.why: An RFP is the most formal procurement document, detailing the problem and required solution for the seller.

distractors.text: Request for Information (RFI)
distractors.trap: An RFI gathers general information and focuses on a specific aspect only.

distractors.text: Request for Quote (RFQ)
distractors.trap: An RFQ seeks pricing and is narrow in scope, often referencing an RFP.

distractors.text: Request for Contract (RFC)
distractors.trap: Not a standard scope-defining procurement document for this purpose.

explanation: An RFP is used when the solution is not obvious and requires the fullest, most formal statement of the problem and requirements, giving a new vendor the best information.

prompts: You are the project manager in an organization implementing agile delivery, and you also serve as an agile coach. A delivery specialist asks what the main focus of a servant leader is. What is your response?

correct.text: The growth and well-being of people and the communities they belong to.
correct.why: Servant leaders measure success through the growth, development, and well-being of their people and communities.

distractors.text: Client satisfaction and demonstrating the value delivered.
distractors.trap: A delivery outcome, not the servant leader's central focus on people.

distractors.text: Managing stakeholder expectations and communication.
distractors.trap: Typical of traditional leadership, not the essence of servant leadership.

distractors.text: Output pace and efficiency to meet the commitments made.
distractors.trap: Prioritizing throughput reflects a traditional, not servant, focus.

explanation: Servant leadership seeks opportunities to serve others and measures success through the growth, development, and well-being of team members and their communities.

prompts: During a daily meeting, the team raises that a business subject matter expert keeps changing the definition of done and worries this signals distrust. Which response fits best?

correct.text: Remind the team that continuous feedback is important for success.
correct.why: In agile the definition of done can evolve, and ongoing feedback drives continuous improvement.

distractors.text: Remind the team to focus on their work and commit to speaking with the SME.
distractors.trap: Telling them to ignore the SME dismisses valid evolving feedback.

distractors.text: Remind the value representative to support the team and speak with the SME.
distractors.trap: Deflecting to the value representative avoids the real point about feedback.

distractors.text: Remind the SME to keep the definition of done originally agreed.
distractors.trap: Freezing the definition of done contradicts agile's adaptive nature.

explanation: In agile the definition of done can change, so the PM reframes the concern by reminding the team that continuous feedback is valuable and works to incorporate the evolving needs collaboratively.

eco_task: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
topic: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
prompts: Over the last five daily syncs on a hybrid predictive-agile project, one team member has been vague about what they have accomplished and what they plan to achieve. Which response fits best?

correct.text: Meet with the team member to ensure they clearly understand what is expected of them.
correct.why: Direct, early conversation clarifies expectations and surfaces any obstacles the member faces.

distractors.text: Examine the issue at the next retrospective and decide on corrective action.
distractors.trap: Waiting lets the problem persist and grow before it is addressed.

distractors.text: Raise the issue of task accountability with the team and stakeholders at the iteration review.
distractors.trap: A public forum feels confrontational and may single out the member.

distractors.text: Facilitate a meeting with the team and stakeholders to review project risks and impediments.
distractors.trap: This is about one member's engagement, not project-wide risks.

explanation: A consistently vague team member should be addressed directly and early; a one-on-one meeting clarifies expectations, reinforces clear communication, and surfaces any obstacles the member faces.

prompts: What are the four team-building leadership styles?

correct.text: Visionary, affiliative, coaching, and participative.
correct.why: These four styles emphasize relationships and developing people, which builds the team.

distractors.text: Visionary, affiliative, coaching, and pace-setting.
distractors.trap: Pace-setting focuses on achievement over relationships, not team building.

distractors.text: Visionary, affiliative, coaching, and directive.
distractors.trap: Directive emphasizes control and results rather than building the team.

distractors.text: Visionary, affiliative, coaching, and high-waters.
distractors.trap: High-waters is not a recognized team-building leadership style.

explanation: Visionary, affiliative, coaching, and participative are the relationship-focused, team-building leadership styles; pace-setting, directive, and high-waters emphasize achievement over relationships.

prompts: A software delivery team at a large bank is frustrated by the heavy documentation the governance review board requires. The project manager approaches the governance department and proposes working together to reduce the paperwork, explaining that it slows the team down and adds overhead. This best illustrates which type of leadership?

correct.text: Servant leadership
correct.why: The PM removes an impediment on the team's behalf, serving their needs rather than commanding.

distractors.text: Transactional leadership
distractors.trap: Assumes leadership through rewards and penalties for performance, not removing obstacles.

distractors.text: Directive leadership
distractors.trap: Implies giving orders and close control, the opposite of enabling the team.

distractors.text: Constructive leadership
distractors.trap: A generic-sounding label; not the recognized style for serving the team.

explanation: By clearing an obstacle for the team and advocating on their behalf with governance, the project manager is demonstrating servant leadership.

prompts: As project manager, you want to motivate your delivery specialists and improve project performance. Which approach is most likely to be effective?

correct.text: Offer a range of incentives tailored to each team member's individual needs.
correct.why: People are motivated by different things at different times, so tailored incentives address diverse needs.

distractors.text: Reward all delivery specialists equally with formal public recognition awards.
distractors.trap: Uniform rewards ignore diverse needs and can demotivate high performers.

distractors.text: Apply initiatives based on the project manager's own view of what motivates people.
distractors.trap: Relying on personal assumptions leads to mismatched, ineffective incentives.

distractors.text: Delegate motivation to the specialists, assuming professionals self-motivate.
distractors.trap: Assumes everyone is intrinsically motivated and ignores individual differences.

explanation: Effective motivation recognizes each person as an individual, since different people are driven by different needs, and tailors incentives accordingly.

eco_task: Promote Team Performance Through the Application of Emotional Intelligence
topic: Promote Team Performance Through the Application of Emotional Intelligence
prompts: A highly talented, results-oriented team member is suddenly struggling with a personal problem. Their behavior has changed, and it is beginning to affect both project deliverables and the morale of other specialists. What is the best response?

correct.text: Approach the team member privately and work together on a plan to address the issue.
correct.why: A private, empathetic conversation tackles the root cause while maintaining trust and morale.

distractors.text: Reassign the team member to work that would not significantly affect deliverables.
distractors.trap: Sidelining them avoids the root cause and may worsen the situation.

distractors.text: Log the team member's problem in the issue tracker and notify key stakeholders.
distractors.trap: Exposing a personal issue publicly breaches trust and does not resolve it.

distractors.text: Ask the HR manager to handle it and continue working as planned.
distractors.trap: Escalating to HR first skips the empathetic direct conversation that should come first.

explanation: The project manager should first address the situation privately and compassionately, collaborating with the team member on a plan that balances their well-being with project needs.

prompts: During a team meeting, two delivery specialists who worked on the same tasks argue over who is at fault for the work being late, each refusing to be seen as the cause of the delay. What is the most likely cause of this conflict?

correct.text: Unclear responsibilities
correct.why: When responsibilities are ambiguous, ownership is disputed and blame conflicts arise.

distractors.text: Conflicting priorities
distractors.trap: Competing priorities cause trade-off disputes, not arguments over fault for one task.

distractors.text: Personal competition
distractors.trap: Assumes rivalry rather than the ambiguity of who owned the work.

distractors.text: Personnel selection
distractors.trap: Blames hiring choices rather than undefined task ownership.

explanation: If responsibilities were clearly defined, ownership would be obvious and blame conflicts less likely, making unclear responsibilities the most probable cause.

prompts: During a weekly meeting, delivery specialists reviewing their assigned tasks complain that there is not enough information to estimate how long the work will take. What is the most likely cause of this issue?

correct.text: Project objectives are not clearly defined.
correct.why: Unclear objectives leave specialists without the scope clarity needed to estimate effort.

distractors.text: The business environment is very dynamic.
distractors.trap: A changing environment affects delivery broadly, not the ability to estimate a defined task.

distractors.text: Internal competition between the delivery specialists.
distractors.trap: Rivalry does not explain a shared lack of estimating information.

distractors.text: Roles and responsibilities are not clearly defined.
distractors.trap: The gap is scope clarity, not who is assigned to which task.

explanation: The specialists lack the scope information needed to estimate, which points to unclear project objectives rather than role assignment or environmental factors.

prompts: At the start of a predictive project, the team agreed that all outgoing information would first be shared with the project manager. Mid-project, a team leader communicated directly with the sponsor, bypassing the project manager, so unfiltered information reached the sponsor and important team updates never reached the project manager. What is the best next step?

correct.text: Remind the team leader to always inform the project manager before sharing information with stakeholders.
correct.why: A ground rule was violated, so the PM directly reminds the individual of the agreed rule.
